title: Where PM Is Used & Its History
intro: This module shows you where project management is used and where it came from. You will see that project management is not only for one kind of job. Business owners, artists, teachers, engineers, nurses, lawyers' helpers, and software makers all use these skills every day. You will also learn a short, friendly history of project management, from ancient walls and pyramids to the tools we still use now, like the Gantt chart. By the end, you will be able to spot project management skills in almost any career, including your own. Let's begin.

steps.title: Project Management Is a Life Skill
steps.body:
Project management is not only for people with the job title "project manager." The skills you learn in project management help in almost any job, and in daily life too.

What are these skills? Strong planning, so you know what to do and when. Good communication, so people understand each other. The ability to carry out a plan and deliver a product or a service. And the ability to watch for risks (things that can go wrong) and manage resources (people, money, time, and tools).

The chapter lists many fields where project managers work. These include farming and natural resources, arts and media, building and construction, energy, engineering, fashion, finance, health, tourism, manufacturing, education, retail, transportation, and information technology (computers). That is a very wide list. It shows that these skills travel well from one field to another.

So, as you study project management, remember this: you are not learning a narrow skill for one job. You are learning a set of skills that gives you an edge, or an advantage, almost anywhere you go.
steps.example: A person who plans a family wedding uses the same skills as a project manager: a budget, a schedule, clear communication, and a plan for problems like bad weather.
keyPoints: PM skills help in most careers and in daily life.
Core skills: planning, communication, delivery, managing risk and resources.
Project managers work in many fields, from farming to software.

steps.title: Business Owners Are Project Managers
steps.body:
Business owners need project management skills, even if they never call themselves project managers. Why? Because every successful business must give the customer what they need.

Think about three things a customer wants. First, quality: the product or service must be good. Second, cost: the price must match what the customer expected. Third, timeliness: the product or service must arrive on time, by the deadline.

The chapter calls these ideas the "pillars" of project management: delivering within schedule, cost, scope, and quality. "Scope" means the amount and type of work you agreed to do. To hit all four pillars, a business owner needs many skills. They must plan, organize, and set the scope. They must analyze problems, communicate, make a budget, hire staff, buy equipment, carry out the work, and deliver the result.

Owners also need to understand money: the finances, the daily operations, and the expenses. Some businesses focus more on one area, like accounting, sales, training, or public relations. An owner might run a travel agency, a hotel, or a small shop in the town market. In every case, the owner is really managing a project every single day.
steps.example: A small bakery owner plans the day's baking (schedule), keeps prices fair (cost), decides what to sell (scope), and makes sure the bread tastes good (quality).
keyPoints: The four pillars are schedule, cost, scope, and quality.
"Scope" means the agreed amount and type of work.
Owners plan, budget, hire, buy, deliver, and track money.

steps.title: Example: The Restaurant Manager
steps.body:
A restaurant manager runs a restaurant every day. This is a great example of project management in action.

First, planning. The manager must coordinate many departments: the kitchen, the dining room, banquet events, and the vendors who bring supplies. Good planning keeps customers happy. The manager also schedules staff and the timing of food deliveries. If these are late, customers wait too long.

Second, people skills. The manager must hire and keep good employees, watch their work, and train them. This keeps quality high while keeping costs under control.

Third, risk management. Food safety is a real risk. The manager watches kitchen orders to find delays and works with the chef to stop them. The law is also a risk: the restaurant must be clean and must serve alcohol legally, or it can be closed.

Fourth, money. The manager or chef studies recipes to find the food, labor, and overhead costs. They set portion sizes and prices so supplies can be ordered in time. They also plan routine jobs, like deep cleaning and pest control, for slow times or when the restaurant is closed.

A good restaurant depends on the very skills project management teaches.
steps.example: The manager schedules the heavy kitchen cleaning for a closed day, so it does not disturb paying customers.
keyPoints: Restaurant managers use planning, people skills, risk control, and money skills.
Scheduling staff and deliveries keeps customers satisfied.
Legal and food-safety rules are risks the manager must manage.

steps.title: Quiz: The Four Pillars
steps.body: Let's check one key idea before we move on.

quiz.question: The chapter says the "pillars" of project management are delivering a product or service within four limits. Which four?
options: Schedule, cost, scope, and quality
Speed, size, style, and staff
Planning, hiring, cleaning, and selling
Marketing, sales, taxes, and profit
quiz.explanation: The four pillars named in the chapter are schedule, cost, scope, and quality.

steps.title: Construction Managers and Outsourcing
steps.body:
Construction managers plan, direct, and budget building projects. These can be homes, shops, offices, roads, bridges, schools, or hospitals. A construction manager may also be called a project manager or project engineer.

Scheduling skills are very important here. So is communication, because the manager must coordinate designers, work teams, and special trades like carpentry, plumbing, and electrical wiring. They also talk with government officials to get permits (official permission to build).

The manager makes sure the project finishes on time and on budget, meets quality rules and building codes, and stays safe. To do this, they break the whole job into logical steps and estimate the time each step needs. Many use software such as Microsoft Project or online tools, and most rely on spreadsheets. They also handle procurement, which means buying materials like lumber for a house.

The chapter also mentions outsourcing. This means paying an outside company to do a service for you. When a business plans outsourcing, it reviews its finances, talks with stakeholders (people affected by the project), and checks time, cost, schedule, scope, and quality. A simple status chart, often using red, yellow, and green colors, helps the whole team see how the project is going.
steps.example: A red-yellow-green status chart shows a road project at a glance: green tasks are fine, yellow need attention, and red tasks are in trouble.
keyPoints: Construction managers may be called project managers or project engineers.
"Procurement" means buying the materials and services a project needs.
A red-yellow-green chart shows project status quickly to everyone.

steps.title: Teach Back: Owners and Builders
steps.body:
Time to teach, because teaching helps you remember. Imagine a friend asks you, "Why do business owners and construction managers need project management skills?"

In your own simple words, explain at least three skills they share. Think about planning, scheduling, budgeting, communication, risk, and buying materials (procurement). Try to give one small example for each skill, like the restaurant manager or the road builder.
steps.example: You might say: "Both make a plan, both watch the budget, and both must talk clearly with their teams. A builder buys lumber; a restaurant owner buys food."
keyPoints: Name at least three shared skills.
Give a small example for each.
Use plain words, as if teaching a friend.

steps.title: Creative Services: Graphic Artists
steps.body:
Creative jobs also use project management. These include graphic artists, video editors, game managers, media producers, technical writers, translators, and more. They use these skills to deliver their work and meet what the client asks for.

Let's look at graphic artists. They create visual solutions to communication problems. In simple words, they find the best way to send a message using color, type, pictures, photography, and animation. Their work appears in magazines, newspapers, company reports, logos, packaging, websites, TV, movies, and phone apps.

Where is the project management? At the start, a designer must find out the client's needs, the message the design should send, and how it will appeal to customers. This first step is like "initiation" in project management, where you define what the project is about. The designer also thinks about the audience's culture and background, just like a project manager thinks about different stakeholders.

Designers gather information by meeting clients, brainstorming, and doing research. This helps them keep quality high and manage risks. They may also lead assistants who do part of the work. So they use scheduling, resource planning, and cost control, all pillars of project management.
steps.example: A designer meets a bakery owner, learns the brand should feel warm and friendly, then plans, schedules, and delivers a new logo on time and on budget.
keyPoints: "Initiation" means the first step, where you define the project and its needs.
Designers manage clients like stakeholders, thinking about culture and audience.
They use scheduling, resource planning, and cost control.

steps.title: Educators and Teachers
steps.body:
"Educator" is a broad word. It can mean a teacher, a lecturer, a professor, a tutor, or a homeschool parent. It can include religious leaders and trainers who teach a skill, like driving a car or using a computer. All educators use planning and communication, which are project management skills.

Teachers are a clear example, because we all had teachers. A teacher acts as a facilitator or coach. A facilitator is someone who helps a group work and learn together. Teachers plan lessons, carry out those plans, and then check each student's progress. This is very close to how a project manager plans a project, carries it out, and monitors the results to deliver something.

Teachers also use "soft skills." Soft skills are people skills, like listening, encouraging, and helping people work together. Teachers use these to manage students, parents, and school leaders. They often ask students to work in groups to solve problems as a team, which builds collaboration.

Some teachers even help make decisions about the budget, staff, textbooks, and lesson design. These are the same skills a project manager uses: managing money and making good decisions.
steps.example: A teacher plans a month of lessons, teaches them, gives quizzes to monitor progress, and adjusts the plan if many students struggle, just like a project manager checking status.
keyPoints: A "facilitator" helps a group learn and work together.
"Soft skills" are people skills like listening and encouraging.
Teachers plan, deliver, and monitor progress, like project managers.

steps.title: Quiz: Skills in Creative and Teaching Jobs
steps.body: Let's review what creative and teaching careers have in common with project management.

quiz.question: In project management, what does "initiation" mean, as shown by the graphic artist example?
options: The first step, where you define the project and the client's needs
The final step, where you deliver the invoice
The step where you fire team members
A type of software for drawing logos
quiz.explanation: Initiation is the starting step, where the designer defines the needs, the message, and the goal of the project.

steps.title: Engineers
steps.body:
Engineers use science and mathematics to solve technical problems in a practical, low-cost way. They take scientific discoveries and turn them into products and services that people and companies can actually use.

Engineers use many project management skills. One important task is to specify functional requirements. This means writing down exactly what the product must do. Clear requirements help everyone build the right thing.

Engineers also care about quality. They judge a design by its effectiveness, cost, reliability, and safety. This is a lot like a project manager who checks whether a product meets the customer's rules before it is accepted and delivered.

Another shared skill is estimation. To estimate means to make a smart guess about numbers before you know them for sure. Engineers are often asked to estimate the time and the cost needed to finish a project. Good estimates help a team plan the schedule and the budget. If estimates are too low, the project can run out of time or money. So engineers, like project managers, must estimate carefully and honestly.
steps.example: An engineer designing a bridge estimates it will take 18 months and a set budget, then checks the design for safety, cost, and reliability before approval.
keyPoints: "Functional requirements" describe exactly what a product must do.
Engineers judge designs by effectiveness, cost, reliability, and safety.
"Estimation" is a careful guess of time and cost before the work is done.

steps.title: Health Care and Radiology
steps.body:
Health care has many jobs that use project management skills. These include athletic trainers, dental hygienists, massage therapists, nurses, physicians, and X-ray technicians. All of them use risk management, because they must never harm the person they care for.

Let's look at radiology technologists. They take medical images like X-rays, CT scans, and MRIs. These images help doctors find problems, such as a broken bone.

Where is the project management? First, people skills and communication. The technologist prepares the patient by explaining the procedure and the correct position to stand or lie in. Second, risk management. Radiation can be dangerous, so they use lead shields and limit the size of the X-ray beam to prevent unnecessary exposure. Third, quality. They check the image and set the machine to get the right detail and contrast.

Safety and regulations are tracked and reported as a control, to prove the team follows the rules. Technologists also use project management to make work schedules, choose equipment to buy, and manage a whole department. Above all, they balance the project triangle of cost, schedule, and scope, with quality, to reach the goal.
steps.example: Before an X-ray, the technologist explains the steps, places a lead shield to reduce radiation risk, and checks the image quality before sending it to the doctor.
keyPoints: Health care workers use risk management to avoid harming patients.
Radiology uses communication, risk control, and quality checks.
The project triangle is cost, schedule, and scope, delivered with quality.

steps.title: Example: Nurses
steps.body:
Nurses treat and educate patients, families, and the public. They also give advice and emotional support. Their work shows clear project management skills.

A nurse creates a care plan for each patient. This plan is like a project plan. It includes scheduling when to start and stop medications, such as IV lines for fluids or medicine, and when to apply treatments. The nurse communicates with the patient, the family, physicians, and other health workers. This can happen in person or through technology. "Telehealth" means giving care and advice through electronic tools like video calls, the internet, or the telephone.

Risk management is very important for a nurse. Some cases are a matter of life and death. Nurses watch pain levels and vital signs, like heart rate, and send status reports to physicians so the team can respond quickly.

The nursing field is wide. Some nurses work in infection control. They track and stop the spread of infections and plan for outbreaks. Others are educators who design and evaluate training programs. Nurses may also give consultations, advise on public policy, do research, or support the sale of a product. In every role, they plan, monitor, and deliver care.
steps.example: A nurse schedules a patient's medicine times, checks vital signs, and reports a sudden change to the doctor right away, managing a real life-or-death risk.
keyPoints: A nurse's "care plan" works like a project plan.
"Telehealth" is care given through video, internet, or phone.
Nurses schedule treatments, monitor risks, and report status.

steps.title: Teach Back: Risk Management in Health Care
steps.body:
Let's teach again, this time about health care. Imagine a classmate says, "I don't understand risk management. Why does it matter for a nurse or an X-ray technician?"

Explain, in your own simple words, what risk management means and give two real examples from this chapter. Think about the lead shield in radiology and the way a nurse watches vital signs and reports changes fast.
steps.example: You might say: "Risk management means finding dangers early and stopping them. A radiology tech uses a lead shield to block extra radiation. A nurse watches a patient's heart rate and calls the doctor if something looks wrong."
keyPoints: Define risk management in plain words.
Give two examples from health care in this chapter.
Show how catching risk early protects the patient.

steps.title: Paralegals
steps.body:
In a law firm, attorneys (lawyers) hold the final responsibility for legal work, but they get help. Paralegals give this help. They do many tasks to support lawyers. However, paralegals are not allowed to do the work only lawyers may do, such as giving legal advice, setting legal fees, or presenting a case in court.

Paralegals use many project management skills. Planning helps them prepare lawyers for closings, hearings, trials, and meetings. Communication helps them write clear reports and drafts that guide how a case is handled.

Monitoring is another key skill. Paralegals track important case documents and watch filing dates, so nothing is late. This is a form of risk containment, which means keeping risks small and under control. They also use procurement skills when they negotiate to hire expert witnesses or other services.

Finally, paralegals may use financial skills, such as helping prepare tax returns, setting up trust funds, or keeping the firm's financial records. Paralegals work across many kinds of law, including criminal law, family law, immigration, bankruptcy, and real estate. In all of these, project management skills help them stay organized and on time.
steps.example: A paralegal builds a schedule of court filing dates and tracks each document, so the lawyer never misses a deadline, which is risk containment.
keyPoints: Paralegals support lawyers but cannot give legal advice or argue in court.
"Risk containment" means keeping risks small and under control.
They use planning, monitoring, procurement, and financial skills.

steps.title: Software Developers and Science Technicians
steps.body:
Software developers and programmers design and build software. They use computer science and mathematics to create, test, and evaluate programs. Software appears in games, business apps, operating systems, and network systems.

Developers use project management skills in many ways. They gather the requirements for the software, which means the list of what it must do. They identify and track development tasks. They communicate inside the team and with clients. They test cases, and they manage quality, the schedule, and resources like staff, equipment, and labs.

Science technicians also use these skills. They use science and mathematics to help with research and to improve products and processes. They are more practical, or hands-on, than scientists. They set up and run lab instruments, watch experiments, and record results. Quality matters here, just as in project management. For example, food science technicians test additives to make sure they follow government rules for color, texture, and nutrients.

There are many types. Biological technicians help study living things and medicines. Chemical technicians collect and analyze samples in labs or factories. All of them plan, execute, and manage risks, while checking cost, schedule, resources, and quality standards.
steps.example: A software team writes down what an app must do (requirements), splits the work into tasks, tests each part, and tracks the schedule until launch.
keyPoints: "Requirements" are the list of what the software must do.
Developers track tasks, communicate, test, and manage quality and schedule.
Science technicians plan experiments and check quality against set standards.

steps.title: Build: Map Your Own Career
steps.body:
Now make it personal. Choose one job you know well. It can be your current job, a past job, a dream job, or even a daily task like cooking dinner for guests.

Write a short plan that shows the project management skills inside that job. Include these five parts:
1) The goal: what you must deliver.
2) Schedule: two or three time steps or deadlines.
3) Cost: one thing about the budget or resources.
4) Quality: how you know the result is good.
5) Risk: one thing that could go wrong, and how you would manage it.

Keep it short and simple, just a few lines for each part. The goal is to see that you already use project management thinking.
steps.example: For "cook a dinner party": Goal, serve a 3-course meal for six. Schedule, shop Friday, prep Saturday morning, cook by 6 pm. Cost, stay under a set food budget. Quality, food is hot and tasty. Risk, a guest is allergic to nuts, so I check every recipe.
keyPoints: Pick any real job or task you know.
Cover goal, schedule, cost, quality, and risk.
See that you already think like a project manager.

steps.title: A Short History: Ancient Times to the Railroad
steps.body:
Now let's travel back in time. Where did project management come from?

Could the Great Wall of China, the pyramids, or Stonehenge have been built without project management? Probably not. To build such huge things, ancient leaders had to plan bold projects and manage money, materials, and workers, all inside a set time frame. So we can say the idea of project management has existed since the start of history.

But modern project management grew from more recent work. In the late 19th century in the United States, large government projects pushed leaders to make better plans. A key example is the transcontinental railroad, a railway across the whole country, which started construction in the 1860s.

Suddenly, business leaders faced a huge task. They had to organize the manual labor of thousands of workers. They also had to process and assemble huge amounts of raw material, more than anyone had handled before. There were no computers to help. To succeed, they needed new ways to organize, schedule, and coordinate the work. This need for better planning became a foundation for the project management methods we use today.
steps.example: Building the transcontinental railroad meant organizing thousands of workers and massive amounts of steel and wood, which forced leaders to plan on a huge scale.
keyPoints: The idea of project management is as old as ancient wonders.
Large 19th-century projects pushed leaders to plan better.
The transcontinental railroad (1860s) is a key early example.

steps.title: The Gantt Chart and the Manhattan Project
steps.body:
In the 1910s, a man named Henry Gantt studied the order of work in great detail. He became famous for the Gantt chart. A Gantt chart is a popular bar chart that shows a project schedule. Each bar shows a task and how long it lasts. It became a common way to show the phases and activities of a project so that many people can understand it at a glance.

Today the Gantt chart feels normal, but when it first appeared, it was revolutionary, meaning it was a big, new change. Gantt charts were used on huge projects like the Hoover Dam and the interstate highway system. They are still important tools today.

By the middle of the 20th century, projects were often managed in an ad hoc way, which means made up as you go, without a fixed plan, mostly using Gantt charts and informal tools. During this time came the Manhattan Project. This was the secret Allied effort in World War II to build the first nuclear weapons. It was only possible because of project management methods. It involved over 30 sites, employed 130,000 people, and cost nearly two billion US dollars. Its huge size and complexity needed careful coordination.
steps.example: A Gantt chart for building a house shows one bar for the foundation, then a bar for the walls, then the roof, so anyone can see the order and timing.
keyPoints: Henry Gantt created the Gantt chart in the 1910s.
A Gantt chart is a bar chart that shows tasks and their timing.
The huge Manhattan Project relied on project management methods.

steps.title: Quiz: A Piece of History
steps.body: Let's check your memory of the history.

quiz.question: Who is most famous for developing the Gantt chart, and when?
options: Henry Gantt, in the 1910s
Booz-Allen and Hamilton, in the 1950s
Henry Ford, in the 1860s
DuPont Corporation, in the 1960s
quiz.explanation: Henry Gantt studied the order of work and developed the famous Gantt chart in the 1910s.

steps.title: Modern Tools: PERT, CPM, and Today
steps.body:
The 1950s marked the start of the modern project management era. Two mathematical scheduling models were created, and they are still used.

The first is PERT, which stands for Program Evaluation and Review Technique. It was developed for the United States Navy's Polaris missile submarine program. PERT is a method for analyzing the tasks in a project. It looks at the time each task needs, the dependencies among tasks (which task must finish before another can start), and the shortest time to finish the whole project.

The second is CPM, the Critical Path Method. It was developed by DuPont and Remington Rand to manage plant maintenance. The "critical path" is usually the longest full path of tasks in a project. Tasks on this path have no spare time, or "float." If a critical task is late, the whole project is late. CPM helps you find how long a complex project will take and which tasks must stay on time.

By the early 1960s, businesses saw the benefit of organizing work around projects. They understood the need to communicate and connect work across many departments and professions. That idea shaped project management as we know it today.
steps.example: In CPM, if painting can only start after the walls are dry, and the walls are on the critical path, then any delay in drying delays the whole project.
keyPoints: PERT analyzes tasks, their timing, and their dependencies.
"Dependencies" mean one task must finish before another can start.
The "critical path" is the longest path; a late critical task delays everything.

title: What a Project Is
intro: Welcome! This module explains one of the most important ideas in project management: what a project really is. Based on Adrienne Watt's open textbook "Project Management," you will learn what makes something a project, how project work is different from everyday work, the basic process of managing a project, and the skills a good project manager needs. We will use simple English and real examples. By the end, you will be able to spot a project, understand its main features, and know what a project manager brings to the team. Let's begin!

steps.title: What Is a Project?
steps.body:
People have been doing projects since the very start of human life. Long ago, a hunting party went out to get meat for the group. That was a project. It had a clear goal, and it did not last forever. Big projects are old too. The pyramids in Egypt and the Great Wall of China were huge projects in their time.

We use the word "project" a lot in normal talk. A person might say, "My project this weekend is to clean out the garage." Going hunting, building pyramids, and fixing a tap all share some features. Those shared features are what make them projects.

So before we learn how to manage projects well, we must first understand what a project is, and just as important, what it is not. In the next steps, we will look at the special features that make work a project. Understanding these features helps you know when project management tools are needed and when they are not.
steps.example: Cleaning out your garage this weekend is a small project: it has a goal (a tidy garage) and it will end.
keyPoints: People have done projects since ancient times.
A project has a clear goal and does not last forever.
First we must understand what a project is before managing one.

steps.title: Projects Are Temporary
steps.body:
A project has special features that make it different from normal, everyday work. The first feature is that a project is temporary. "Temporary" means it does not go on forever. Every project has a clear start date and a clear end date.

This feature matters a lot. Because a project must finish on time, the team puts a lot of effort into meeting the deadline. To help with this, they make a schedule. A schedule is a plan that shows when each task should start and end.

Projects can be short or long. A project can last only minutes or hours. It can also last days, weeks, months, or even years. What matters is not how long it takes, but that it has a beginning and an end.

Because time is limited, planning becomes very important. If you do not watch the calendar, tasks can slip, and the end date can be missed. So a good project manager keeps a close eye on the schedule from the very first day until the project is done.
steps.example: Building a new company website in three months is temporary: it starts on one date and must finish by a set date.
keyPoints: Temporary means the work has a clear start and end.
Projects can last minutes or years.
A schedule shows when tasks begin and end.

steps.title: Projects Are Unique (Not Operations)
steps.body:
The second key feature is that a project is unique. "Unique" means new; it has not been done in exactly this way before. Maybe something similar was done, but never exactly the same.

Think about Ford, a car company. Each new car model that Ford designs is a project. The models are different, with different features for different customers. Designing an SUV is not the same as designing a luxury car. Those design jobs are unique projects.

But building the same cars again and again on the line is not a project. That is an operation. Operations are ongoing and repeat the same steps to make the same results. They have no end date. The goal of operations is to keep the company running. The goal of a project is to meet its goal and then stop.

So here is the simple difference. Operations are ongoing and repeat. Projects are unique and temporary. When you see work that repeats with no end, that is an operation. When you see new work with a clear finish, that is a project. A project also ends if its goals cannot be met or are no longer needed.
steps.example: Designing a new car model is a project; assembling the same model over and over on the line is an operation.
keyPoints: Unique means new; not done exactly this way before.
Operations are ongoing and repeat the same steps.
Projects are unique and temporary; operations are ongoing.

steps.title: Quiz: Project or Operation?
steps.body: Let's check your understanding. Read the question and pick the best answer.

quiz.question: Which of these is a PROJECT, not an operation?
options: Assembling the same car model on the line every day
Designing a brand-new car model for next year
Answering customer phone calls each day
Cleaning the office every evening
quiz.explanation: Designing a new model is unique and temporary, while the other tasks repeat with no end date.

steps.title: A Formal Definition of a Project
steps.body:
There are many ways to write down what a project is. But they all share the same key ideas we have already learned: temporary and unique.

For people who want an official version, the Project Management Institute, or PMI, gives one. PMI is a well-known group that sets standards for the field. PMI says a project is "a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result."

Let's break that down. "Endeavor" just means an effort or a piece of work. "Temporary" means it has a clear beginning and end, as we learned. The end comes when the goals are met. The end can also come when the project is stopped because the goals will not be met, or when no one needs the project any more.

So the formal PMI definition matches everything we already know. A project is a temporary effort. It makes something unique, whether a product, a service, or a result. And it has a real start and a real finish. You do not need to memorize the exact words, but the two big ideas, temporary and unique, are worth remembering.
steps.example: PMI's short definition: "a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result."
keyPoints: PMI is a group that sets project management standards.
PMI defines a project as temporary and unique.
A project ends when goals are met, cannot be met, or are no longer needed.

steps.title: Three Project Characteristics
steps.body:
When you are not sure if you have a project, there are some things to keep in mind. First, ask: is it a project or an ongoing operation? Second, if it is a project, who are the stakeholders? Stakeholders are the people who care about the project or are affected by it, like the customer or the boss. Third, what features make this work a project?

Here are the three main characteristics of a project:

- Projects are unique.
- Projects are temporary and have a clear beginning and ending date.
- Projects are complete when the goals are met, or when it is clear the project is no longer worth doing.

There is one more important idea. A successful project is one that meets or beats the hopes of the stakeholders. In other words, it is not enough to just finish. The result must make the people who care about it happy. If the customer wanted something and got it, the project worked.

So when you check for a project, look for these three features, and always remember the stakeholders. Keeping them satisfied is the real measure of success.
steps.example: A software team finishes an app on time and on budget, and the client is happy with it: that is a successful project.
keyPoints: Stakeholders are people who care about or are affected by the project.
Projects are unique, temporary, and end when goals are met.
Success means meeting or beating stakeholder expectations.

steps.title: A Real Example: The Kiosk Idea
steps.body:
Let's use the checklist on a real story from the chapter. Imagine the Vice-President (VP) of marketing comes to you with an idea. He wants to put small kiosks, like mini-offices, inside local grocery stores. At these kiosks, customers could sign up for car and home insurance and pay their bills.

He wants the kiosks in 12 stores in a big city by the end of the year. He says senior managers already approved it, and he puts you in charge. Your first question should be: "Is this a project?"

Let's test it:

- Is it unique? Yes. These kiosks do not exist in the stores yet. It is a new way to offer the service.
- Does it have a limited timeframe? Yes. It starts now and must end by next year.
- Can we tell when it is done? Yes. When all kiosks are installed and working, it is finished.
- Can we measure if stakeholders are happy? Yes. Their needs will be written as requirements, then compared with the final result.

Because the answer to every question is yes, this is a project.
steps.example: The kiosk idea is unique, has a deadline, has a clear finish, and can be checked against requirements, so it passes as a project.
keyPoints: Test new work with the project checklist.
Ask: unique? limited time? clear finish? measurable satisfaction?
If all answers are yes, you have a project.

steps.title: Teach It Back: Is It a Project?
steps.body: Now it is your turn to teach. Imagine a friend asks you how to tell if some work is a project. In your own words, explain the four questions you would ask to decide. Use the kiosk story or your own example to make it clear. Try to explain why each question matters, not just list them.
steps.example: You might start: "First I ask if the work is unique, meaning new and not done this way before..."
keyPoints: Explain the four test questions in your own words.
Use a simple example to show each one.
Say why stakeholder satisfaction matters.

steps.title: The Process of Project Management
steps.body:
You have a project. Now what? Many teams make a big mistake here. They just "jump in" and start building right away, with no plan. In the end, the product does not match what the customer wanted. This is a common reason projects fail.

The numbers are serious. One well-known report, the Standish Group CHAOS Report from 2009, studied many projects. It found that only about 32% of projects fully succeeded. Around 44% were "challenged," meaning late, over budget, or missing features. And about 24% failed completely.

So what is the answer? Good project management. Project management is the use of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to meet the project's needs. It is a process, not a single action. The process includes three big steps: planning the work, putting the plan into action, and measuring progress and performance.

Managing a project also means writing down what everyone needs and setting clear goals that all agree on. When everyone knows the goal, it is much easier to keep the team on the right path. Good project management does not remove every problem, but it gives you a steady way to handle whatever comes up.
steps.example: The CHAOS Report found only about 32% of projects fully succeeded, showing why a real plan beats "just do it."
keyPoints: Jumping in with no plan is a top reason projects fail.
Project management uses knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques.
The process is: plan, act on the plan, and measure progress.

steps.title: Constraints: Cost, Scope, Quality
steps.body:
Every project has limits that pull on your attention. These limits are called constraints. There are six of them: cost, scope, quality, risk, resources, and time. Let's look at the first three now.

Cost is the budget approved for the project. It covers all the money needed to deliver the work. A project manager must be careful not to run out of money. For almost every project, cost is a hard limit, and going over budget usually forces some fix.

Scope is what the project is trying to achieve. It is all the work needed to deliver the results, plus the steps used to make them. Scope is the reason and purpose of the project. It answers the question: what are we actually building?

Quality is the set of standards the product must meet to work well. The product must do its job, solve the problem, and give the value people expect. It must also be reliable and have a good finish. Quality is checked through quality assurance (QA), which means checking the work often to make sure it will meet the standards. If you cut quality too much, the results can be poor or even dangerous.
steps.example: If a factory is given a fixed budget but keeps its scope large, something has to give, often quality suffers.
keyPoints: Constraints are limits that compete for your attention.
Cost is the budget; scope is what you are building.
Quality is checked with quality assurance (QA).

steps.title: Constraints: Risk, Resources, Time
steps.body:
Now let's look at the other three constraints: risk, resources, and time.

Risk is about outside events that could hurt your project if they happen. Risk mixes two things: the chance the event will happen, and how bad the effect would be. If both the chance and the effect are high, you should name it as a risk. Then you make a plan early to manage it, before it becomes a crisis.

Resources are what you need to do the project tasks. Resources can be people, equipment, buildings, money, or anything else you can name that the work needs. Without the right resources, tasks take longer or cannot be done.

Time is how long the project takes to finish. Time is often the most missed part of planning. This shows up as late deadlines and unfinished work. To control time well, you must list the tasks, guess how long each takes, put them in the right order, and assign people and resources. A good schedule even allows for holidays and vacations.

All six constraints are connected. When you push on one, another one moves. That is why a project manager must watch them all together.
steps.example: If you do not get enough people (resources), your schedule (time) will stretch because the work takes longer.
keyPoints: Risk mixes the chance of an event with its impact.
Resources are people, equipment, money, and more.
Time is often the most overlooked constraint.

steps.title: The Triple Constraint and Trade-offs
steps.body:
You may have heard the term "triple constraint." In the old, simple view, it meant just three limits: time, cost, and scope. These are the main limits you must watch most closely. People often draw them as a triangle, with one limit on each side.

The important idea is that these limits are linked. If you change one side of the triangle, the other sides change too. The best projects keep the triangle balanced. But keeping it balanced is hard, because projects change a lot.

Here are some examples of trade-offs. If your sponsor wants to add more features (more scope), you will likely need more money or more time. If the budget is cut, you may have to lower the quality or do less work. If you do not get enough resources, your schedule will stretch out.

The chapter compares this to the carnival game "Whac-a-Mole." Each time you push one mole down, another pops up. In the same way, when you fix one constraint, another one causes trouble. The best advice is to rely on your team to keep all the "moles" under control at the same time.
steps.example: If a sponsor adds new features to the scope, you will very likely need more money or a longer schedule to finish.
keyPoints: The triple constraint is time, cost, and scope.
Changing one constraint changes the others.
Balancing them is like Whac-a-Mole; lean on your team.

steps.title: Quiz: Understanding Constraints
steps.body: Let's test your grasp of project constraints.

quiz.question: A sponsor asks you to add more features (more scope) but keep the same deadline. What most likely happens?
options: Nothing changes; scope has no effect on other limits
You will likely need more cost or resources, or quality may drop
The project automatically becomes easier
Time is the only constraint that ever matters
quiz.explanation: Constraints are linked, so adding scope usually forces more cost or resources, or a drop in quality.

steps.title: Teach It Back: The Balancing Act
steps.body: Teach this idea to someone new. In your own simple words, explain the six project constraints and why they are connected. Then give one real trade-off example, such as what happens when the budget is cut. Try to use the triangle or Whac-a-Mole picture to make it easy to remember.
steps.example: You might say: "Imagine a triangle of time, cost, and scope. Push one corner and the others move..."
keyPoints: Name the six constraints in your own words.
Explain why they are connected.
Give one clear trade-off example.

steps.title: What Expertise a PM Needs
steps.body:
To manage all these constraints and the whole project, a project manager should bring some areas of expertise to the team. The chapter names four main areas.

First is knowledge of the application area, plus the standards and rules of your industry. Second is understanding of the project environment. Third is general management knowledge and skills. Fourth is interpersonal skills, which means skills for working with people.

Here is an important point. You do not need to be the top technical expert in the field. Your job is the expertise to run the project, not to do every technical task. Your team will have the deep specialists.

For example, imagine you manage a project to build an oil platform. You are not expected to know all the engineering details. Your team will have mechanical and civil engineers for that. Still, it helps if you understand the type of work. That understanding lets you make better choices and talk clearly with your experts.

In the next steps, we will look at each of these areas more closely, so you can see what to build in yourself and what to lean on your team for.
steps.example: A project manager on an oil platform relies on the team's engineers but still benefits from understanding the work.
keyPoints: Four areas: application knowledge, project environment, management skills, people skills.
The PM's expertise is running the project, not every technical task.
The team provides the deep technical knowledge.

steps.title: Application Knowledge and Environment
steps.body:
Let's look at the first two areas of expertise.

Application knowledge means knowing the standards and rules of your field. Standards are guidelines or preferred ways of working; they are not always required. Regulations are rules you must follow, like government laws. As a professional, you must follow the laws for your industry. Application areas are groups of projects with common parts. They can be grouped by industry (like pharmaceuticals or finance), by department (like accounting or legal), by technology (like software), or by specialty. Knowing which rules affect your project before you start helps the work go smoothly and helps you spot risks.

Understanding the project environment is the second area. Here you think about culture, society, and physical factors like time zones. Cultural and international factors are often the hardest and the most ignored. For example, Americans often use first names quickly, while Europeans are more formal and use surnames. Colors also carry different meanings: white means purity in North America but can mean death in Japan. Even date formats differ, so 2/8/2009 can mean February 8 or 2 August depending on the country. A good project manager learns these customs before leading an international project.
steps.example: Call-waiting was popular in the West but seen as impolite in parts of Asia, a costly culture mistake for phone companies.
keyPoints: Standards are preferred guidelines; regulations are required rules.
Application areas group projects by industry, department, or technology.
Culture, colors, and date formats change how work is received.

steps.title: Management and People Skills
steps.body:
The last two areas of expertise are management skills and interpersonal skills.

Management knowledge and skills are your ability to plan the project, run it well, control it, and bring it to a good finish. In many ways, running a project is like running a small business. There are risks and rewards, money and accounting tasks, human resource issues, time management, and stress management. These general management skills are needed in every project.

Interpersonal skills are your ability to work with people and handle personnel issues as they come up. These are people skills, and they are just as important as technical knowledge.

One people skill stands out: communication. Project managers spend about 90% of their time communicating. So they must be good communicators who share clear information. Your staff must know what to do, when to do it, and what budget, time, and quality limits they face. If staff do not know their tasks, the project stops. If you do not know what the team is doing, you cannot track progress. Communication can be summed up as knowing "who needs what information and when," and making sure they get it.
steps.example: Because project managers spend about 90% of their time communicating, clear updates keep the whole team moving.
keyPoints: Management skills: plan, run, control, and finish the project well.
Interpersonal skills mean working well with people.
Communication is huge: PMs spend about 90% of their time on it.

steps.title: More People Skills: Leading and Solving
steps.body:
Beyond communication, a project manager needs several more interpersonal skills. Let's look at five of them.

Influence is understanding the hidden forces in an organization. Every workplace has politics, different motivations, and power struggles. A good PM sees these unspoken forces and works with them to get things done.

Leadership is the ability to motivate and inspire people toward a goal. Leaders share a vision and bring people together around common goals. A good PM inspires the team to see the value of the project and to overcome obstacles.

Motivation keeps people working well and producing good results. It is a constant job. Team-building activities, plus recognition and rewards, help keep the team going. Remember that some people love praise in front of others, while some do not, so match the reward to the person.

Negotiation means reaching an agreement that all sides can accept. A PM must negotiate with stakeholders, vendors, and customers for the good of the project.

Problem solving means finding the real cause of a problem and choosing a solution. Start by defining the problem and doing root-cause analysis. If you treat only the symptom, the problem returns, and doing nothing is usually the worst choice of all.
steps.example: Raising a car fuse's rating stops the blown fuse but ignores the real electrical short; root-cause analysis finds the true problem.
keyPoints: Influence: understand hidden politics and power in the organization.
Leadership and motivation inspire and energize the team.
Problem solving means finding the root cause, not just the symptom.

steps.title: Quiz: Interpersonal Skills
steps.body: Let's check what you remember about a project manager's people skills.

quiz.question: A project manager keeps fixing the symptom of a problem, and it keeps coming back. Which skill is missing?
options: Problem solving with root-cause analysis
Typing faster on the computer
Buying more equipment
Working longer hours alone
quiz.explanation: Root-cause analysis finds the true cause, so treating only symptoms means the problem-solving skill is missing.

steps.title: Build: Check Your Own Project
steps.body:
Time to practice. Think of a real piece of work in your life, school, or job. It could be planning an event, building a small website, or moving to a new office.

Write a short note that does three things:
1. Use the project checklist to decide if it is a project. Is it unique? Does it have a start and end date? Can you tell when it is done? Can you measure if stakeholders are happy?
2. Name the six constraints for your work: cost, scope, quality, risk, resources, and time. Write one sentence for each.
3. List two interpersonal skills (like communication or negotiation) you would use most, and say why.

Keep it short and simple. This exercise helps you connect the lesson to real life.
steps.example: For "plan a birthday party": unique yes, end date yes; cost = budget for food; time = the party date; top skill = communication with guests.
keyPoints: Apply the project checklist to real work.
Write one sentence for each of the six constraints.
Pick two people skills you would rely on most.

title: The Project Life Cycle
intro: Every project has a start, a middle, and an end. In this module you will learn the four phases that most projects follow: initiation, planning, implementation (execution), and closing. Together these phases are called the "project life cycle." You will see what happens in each phase, why the order matters, and how the phases connect on a big real-world project. The lessons use short, simple English, with examples, quick quizzes, and small tasks to help the ideas stick.

steps.title: What is a project life cycle?
steps.body:
A project is a piece of work with a clear goal. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. The end can be a success or a failure, but there is always an end.

Most projects move through four big phases. Each phase has its own tasks and its own problems to solve. The four phases are: initiation, planning, implementation (also called execution), and closing.

When we put these four phases together, they show the full path of a project from start to finish. We call this path the "project life cycle." Think of it like the life of a plant: a seed is planted, the plant grows, it gives fruit, and then the season ends.

The project manager and the project team share one goal: to do the work of the project so that the project meets its objectives. An "objective" is simply the result you want. Knowing the life cycle helps the team know what to do first, what to do next, and when the project is truly done.
steps.example: Building a new website: first you decide you need it (initiation), then you plan the pages and budget (planning), then you build it (implementation), and finally you hand it over and review it (closing).
keyPoints: A project always has a beginning, a middle, and an end.
The four phases are initiation, planning, implementation, and closing.
Together the phases are called the project life cycle.

steps.title: The four phases at a glance
steps.body:
Before we look at each phase in detail, let's see all four together. This gives you a map for the whole module.

Initiation is the start. Here the team decides what the project is and if it is worth doing. Planning comes next. Here the team works out all the details: the tasks, the schedule, the money, and the risks.

Implementation (or execution) is the middle. This is where the real work happens and the plan is put into action. Closing is the end. Here the team hands over the results, closes contracts, and looks back to learn lessons.

Each phase leads into the next. You cannot plan well until you know what the project is. You cannot execute well until you have a plan. And you cannot close until the work is done and accepted. The phases build on each other, like steps on a staircase.
steps.example: For a school event: decide to hold it (initiation), plan the date, tasks, and cost (planning), run the event (implementation), then clean up and review what went well (closing).
keyPoints: Initiation = decide what and why.
Planning = work out the details.
Implementation = do the work.
Closing = finish, hand over, and learn.

steps.title: Phase 1: Initiation
steps.body:
The initiation phase is the very start of the project. Here the team finds the project objective or need. This need can be a business problem to fix or a new opportunity to take.

The team writes a "business case." This is a short document that explains the need and suggests possible solutions. Then the team does a "feasibility study." This study checks each solution to see if it can really meet the goal. It asks two key questions: "Can we do the project?" (feasibility) and "Should we do the project?" (justification).

Once the best solution is chosen and approved, the project officially starts. A project manager is appointed to lead it. The team also names the major deliverables (the main things the project must produce) and the work groups who will help. Slowly, the project team starts to take shape.

At the end of initiation, the project manager asks for approval to move on to detailed planning.
steps.example: A shop loses sales because its checkout is slow. In initiation, the manager writes a business case, studies a few fixes, and gets approval to build a faster checkout system.
keyPoints: The need or problem is identified and written in a business case.
A feasibility study asks: can we do it, and should we do it?
A project manager is appointed and the team begins to form.

steps.title: Quick check: initiation questions
steps.body: In the initiation phase, the team asks two key questions.

quiz.question: In the initiation phase, what do the questions "feasibility" and "justification" mean?
options: "Can we do the project?" and "Should we do the project?"
"How much will it cost?" and "Who will pay?"
"When is it due?" and "Who is on the team?"
"Did it succeed?" and "What did we learn?"
quiz.explanation: Feasibility asks if the project is possible (can we do it), and justification asks if it is worth doing (should we do it).

steps.title: Phase 2: Planning the work, schedule, and budget
steps.body:
The planning phase is where the chosen solution is developed in as much detail as possible. The team plans every step needed to meet the project's goal.

First, the team identifies all of the work to be done. They list the tasks and the resources they will need, plus the plan for producing them. This work of defining exactly what is and is not part of the project is called "scope management." "Scope" means the full set of work in the project.

Next, the team creates a project plan. This plan shows the activities, the tasks, the "dependencies" (which tasks must finish before others can start), and the time-frames. It acts like a road map for the whole team.

Then the project manager prepares a budget. A budget is a plan for the money. The manager gives cost estimates for labor (the people), equipment, and materials. Later, during the work, this budget is used to watch and control spending. When the work, the schedule, and the costs are ready, the three main parts of planning are complete.
steps.example: For an app project, the team lists every feature, draws a schedule showing which features come first, and estimates the cost of developers, servers, and design tools.
keyPoints: Scope management defines all the work to be done.
The project plan shows tasks, dependencies, and time-frames.
The budget estimates labor, equipment, and material costs.
Work, schedule, and costs are the three main parts of planning.

steps.title: Phase 2 continued: risk, stakeholders, and quality
steps.body:
After the work, schedule, and budget are ready, planning is not finished yet. Now is a great time to look for anything that could threaten the project. This work is called "risk management."

In risk management, the team finds "high-threat" problems that might happen. For each one, they decide what action to take. The action can either lower the chance the problem happens, or lower the harm to the project if it does happen. Planning ahead like this saves stress later.

This is also a good time to find all the "stakeholders." A stakeholder is any person or group with an interest in the project. The team makes a "communication plan" that says what information each stakeholder needs and how it will reach them.

Finally, the team writes a quality plan, with quality targets and checks. They also write an acceptance plan, which lists the exact criteria the result must meet for the customer to accept it. Now the project is planned in full detail and is ready to be executed.
steps.example: For an outdoor concert, the team notes rain as a high-threat risk and books a backup tent. They also list the mayor, sponsors, and neighbors as stakeholders to keep informed.
keyPoints: Risk management finds high-threat problems and plans actions for them.
Stakeholders are people with an interest in the project.
A communication plan keeps stakeholders informed.
Quality and acceptance plans define what "good" and "done" mean.

steps.title: Teach it back: why plan before you build?
steps.body: Imagine a friend wants to skip planning and start the work right away. In your own words, explain why the planning phase matters. Try to mention at least three things a good plan includes (for example: the list of work, the schedule, the budget, risks, or the communication plan). What could go wrong if the team skips planning?

steps.title: Phase 3: Implementation (execution)
steps.body:
The implementation phase is also called the execution phase. Here the project plan is put into motion and the real work of the project is done. This is the phase where the project comes to life.

During this phase, people carry out the tasks from the plan. A construction team lays bricks, a software team writes code, an event team sets up the stage. Progress information is shared through regular team meetings, so everyone knows how the work is going.

The project manager spends most of their time in this phase. Their main job here is to keep control. It is important to keep communicating and to keep the work on track.

Each deliverable that the team produces should be reviewed for quality. The team measures it against the acceptance criteria they wrote during planning. This makes sure the result is good enough before it moves forward. Doing the work and checking the work happen side by side.
steps.example: For a mobile app, developers build each feature during execution, test it against the agreed quality checks, and report progress in a weekly team meeting.
keyPoints: The plan is put into action and the work is performed.
The project manager spends most of their time in this phase.
Progress is shared through regular team meetings.
Deliverables are checked against the acceptance criteria.

steps.title: Staying in control during execution
steps.body:
Even the best plan will not go perfectly. During execution, the project manager watches progress all the time and makes changes when needed. This is how the project stays under control.

The manager compares the progress reports with the project plan. This shows how the project is really doing. If the work drifts away from the plan, this gap is called a "variance." A variance is a difference between what was planned and what is actually happening.

When there is a variance, the first choice is always to bring the project back on course, meaning back to the original plan. If that is not possible, the team records the changes from the original plan and then publishes the updated plan so everyone has the new version.

Status reports should always look ahead to the expected end point in terms of cost, schedule, and quality. Sponsors and other key stakeholders are kept informed at the agreed times and in the agreed format. When all deliverables are done and the customer accepts the final result, the project is ready for closure.
steps.example: A builder falls two weeks behind. The manager first tries to catch up. When that fails, they record the delay as a variance, update the schedule, and share the new plan with the client.
keyPoints: A variance is a difference between the plan and the real progress.
First try to return the project to the original plan.
If not possible, record the changes and publish the updated plan.
Keep stakeholders informed of cost, schedule, and quality.

steps.title: Quick check: handling a variance
steps.body: During execution, the work drifts away from the plan.

quiz.question: When the project drifts from the plan (a variance appears), what should the team try FIRST?
options: Cancel the project
Bring the project back on course to the original plan
Write the lessons-learned report
Skip the next team meeting
quiz.explanation: The first course of action is always to bring the project back to the original plan; only if that fails does the team record and publish changes.

steps.title: Teach it back: the manager's job in execution
steps.body: A new team member asks: "What does the project manager actually do during the execution phase?" In your own words, explain their main job. Try to include how the manager keeps control, how they use progress reports, and what they do when the work does not match the plan.

steps.title: Phase 4: Closing the project
steps.body:
The closing phase is the final part of the life cycle. It is also called the completion phase. Many people forget this phase, but it is very important. It makes sure the project ends in a clean and organized way.

During closing, the team releases the final deliverables to the customer. They hand over the project documents to the business, so the information is kept safe for the future. They also end supplier contracts, because the outside help is no longer needed.

Next, the team releases the project resources. This means the people and equipment are freed to work on other projects. Finally, the team tells all stakeholders that the project is now closed. This clear ending stops confusion about whether the project is still running.

Closing well shows respect for everyone's time and money. It leaves the customer with a finished result and the team ready to move on.
steps.example: A web agency finishes a client's website, hands over all files and passwords, ends its hosting contract with a supplier, frees its designers for the next job, and emails the client that the project is officially complete.
keyPoints: Release the final deliverables to the customer.
Hand over documents and end supplier contracts.
Release the people and equipment for other work.
Tell all stakeholders the project is closed.

steps.title: Lessons learned: closing the loop
steps.body:
There is one more important step in the closing phase: the "lessons-learned" study. This is a review of the whole project after it ends.

In this study, the team looks back and asks two simple questions: What went well? And what did not go well? They talk honestly about the good parts and the hard parts. The goal is not to blame anyone. The goal is to learn.

Why does this matter? Because the wisdom from this project is passed back to the organization. Future project teams can then read these lessons and avoid the same mistakes. They can also copy the things that worked well.

In this way, each project makes the next project a little easier. A team that never reviews its work will keep making the same errors. A team that studies its lessons keeps getting better over time. This is how experience turns into real knowledge for the whole company.
steps.example: After a software launch, the team meets and notes that testing started too late (a problem to fix) but daily stand-up meetings kept everyone aligned (a habit to keep). They save these notes for the next project.
keyPoints: Lessons-learned asks what went well and what did not.
The aim is to learn, not to blame.
The wisdom is passed to future project teams.
Good reviews make each new project easier.

steps.title: Quick check: what happens at closing?
steps.body: The project work is done and accepted.

quiz.question: Which task belongs to the closing phase?
options: Writing the feasibility study
Estimating the budget for labor and materials
Handing over deliverables and doing a lessons-learned study
Choosing the project manager
quiz.explanation: Closing includes releasing the final deliverables, handing over documents, and reviewing lessons learned; the other options belong to initiation or planning.

steps.title: How the phases connect on a big project
steps.body:
On a large project, the four phases still apply, but each one is much bigger. Let's follow a real example. A U.S. construction company won a contract to design and build the first copper mine in northern Argentina. There was no existing infrastructure for mining or big construction in that remote area.

During the initiation phase, the project manager focused on finding a strong leadership team with the right knowledge and experience for such a complex, faraway project. The team set up three offices: one in Chile, where mining-construction know-how already existed, and two in Argentina, to build local relationships and expertise.

With the offices in place, the start-up team began creating procedures for getting work done, getting the right permits, and building relationships with Chilean and Argentine partners. Notice how the initiation phase here is not just paperwork. On a large project it can mean hiring leaders, opening offices, and forming partnerships across countries. The size grows, but the purpose of the phase stays the same: define the project and get ready.
steps.example: In the copper-mine project, initiation meant building a leadership team and opening three offices in two countries before any digging began.
keyPoints: On big projects each phase is larger, but the purpose is the same.
Initiation here meant building a leadership team and opening offices.
Early relationships and permits set up the whole project.

steps.title: The big project: planning, execution, and closeout
steps.body:
Let's follow the same copper-mine project through its other phases. During the planning phase, the team built one integrated schedule that linked the design, procurement, and construction teams. "Procurement" means buying the equipment and materials. The controls team made a detailed budget to track spending against the expected costs. The design team turned the early idea into detailed drawings, and the procurement team used those drawings to order materials and plan labor. On a project this size, planning never fully stops, but this phase created enough detail for the teams to coordinate.

During the implementation phase, the team did the real work in the plan and adjusted when things changed. Equipment and materials arrived, workers were hired and trained, and everything from the first bulldozer to the final light switch was installed.

The closeout phase meant handing the finished plant to the client's operations team. A short "punch list" of last items was completed. One office was closed, another archived all the documents, and the Chilean office was already starting the next project. The books were closed, final reports were shared, and the project manager moved on.
steps.example: At closeout, the team finished a punch list of small remaining items, archived every document in the Buenos Aires office, and handed the working plant to the client.
keyPoints: Planning linked design, procurement, and construction in one schedule.
Execution built everything from the first bulldozer to the last switch.
Closeout handed over the plant, finished a punch list, and archived documents.
The phases flow in order, each one building on the last.

steps.title: Map your own mini-project
steps.body: Pick a small project from your own life. It can be planning a trip, organizing a party, or launching a simple website. Write down four short sections, one for each phase. Under Initiation, write why you want to do it and if it is worth doing. Under Planning, list the main tasks, a rough schedule, a budget, and one risk. Under Implementation, write how you will do the work and check progress. Under Closing, write how you will finish, hand over, and note one lesson learned. Aim for just two or three lines per phase. This turns the theory into a plan you could really use.

steps.title: Spot the phase
steps.body:
Test your understanding with a quick sorting task. Below are six real project actions. Write each one next to its phase (initiation, planning, implementation, or closing):
1) The team writes a business case and feasibility study.
2) A worker installs the final light switch and reports progress.
3) The manager estimates the cost of labor and materials.
4) The team runs a lessons-learned study and archives documents.
5) The manager compares progress reports to the plan and fixes a variance.
6) A project manager is appointed and the team begins to form.
After sorting, check your answers against the module: items 1 and 6 are initiation, item 3 is planning, items 2 and 5 are implementation, and item 4 is closing.

title: The PM Framework
intro: Welcome to "The PM Framework." In this module you will learn the big picture of how project management works. First, we look at the framework that guides project managers all over the world: the PMBOK and its 10 knowledge areas. Then we study the most famous idea in project management, the triple constraint: scope, time, and cost, plus quality in the middle. You will see how these four things pull against each other, and why changing one always affects the others. Next, we look at how a company is organized (functional, matrix, or projectized) and how that shapes the power a project manager has. Finally, we learn the difference between a project and normal daily operations. Everything here is grounded in Chapter 4 of "Project Management" by Adrienne Watt. The language is kept simple, with clear examples and short quizzes so you can check your learning. By the end, you will understand the shape of the whole field and be ready to go deeper into each part. Let's begin.

steps.title: What is a project management framework?
steps.body:
A framework is a set of ideas and tools that help you do a big job in an organized way. Think of it like a recipe book for projects. It does not tell you every small step, but it shows you the main parts you must not forget.

In project management, the most widely used framework comes from the Project Management Institute, or PMI. Five volunteers started PMI in 1969 so that project managers could share what they learned. Today PMI is the best-known project management group in the world. It has members in more than 170 countries.

PMI created a book called the Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge. People shorten this long name to PMBOK. First published in 1987, the PMBOK collects the shared knowledge that project managers need. Over one million copies are now in use.

Why does this matter to you? A common framework means project managers from different jobs, like software or construction, can speak the same language. The tools of project management are similar even when the projects are very different.
steps.example: A software project manager and a bridge project manager have very different work. But both use the same PMBOK ideas, like managing scope, time, and cost. The shared framework lets them understand each other.
keyPoints: A framework gives the main parts of a job in an organized way.
PMI is the best-known project management group in the world.
PMBOK is PMI's book of shared project management knowledge.
A common framework lets managers from different industries use the same tools.

steps.title: The 10 knowledge areas of PMBOK
steps.body:
The PMBOK divides all project work into 10 knowledge areas. A knowledge area is simply one part of the job that a project manager must handle well. If you are strong in all 10, you have a good chance to succeed.

Here are the 10 areas in simple words: integration (keeping the whole project working together), scope (deciding what work is in and out), time (planning the schedule), cost (managing the money), quality (making sure the result is good enough), human resources (finding and leading the team), communication (keeping people informed), risk (planning for problems), procurement (buying goods and hiring outside help), and stakeholders (managing the people affected by the project).

You do not need to memorize all 10 today. The point is to see that a project has many moving parts. A good project manager watches all of them at the same time, not just one.

Later modules will explore each area in depth. For now, notice that some of these areas form the core of every project plan: scope, time, and cost. We will focus on those next, because they are the heart of the framework.
steps.example: On a house-building project, the manager must choose the rooms (scope), set the finish date (time), watch the budget (cost), pick good materials (quality), hire builders (human resources), and buy bricks (procurement). All 10 areas appear at once.
keyPoints: PMBOK splits project work into 10 knowledge areas.
Each area is one part of the job the manager must handle.
Scope, time, and cost are the core of every project.
A good manager watches all areas at the same time.

steps.title: The triple constraint: the heart of the framework
steps.body:
The most famous idea in project management is the triple constraint. A constraint is a limit, something that holds you back or ties your hands. Every project has three main constraints: scope, time, and cost.

Scope is the work you promise to do. Time is how long you have. Cost is how much money you can spend. People often draw these three as a triangle, with one constraint on each side.

Here is the key idea: the three are connected. If you change one, you usually must change the others. This is why the triangle is such a useful picture. You cannot pull one corner without moving the shape.

For example, if a client asks for more work (bigger scope) but keeps the same finish date, you will likely need more money for extra workers. If the client wants to save money (lower cost), you may have to do less work or take more time.

Understanding this trade-off is one of the most important skills a project manager can learn. When someone asks for a change, your job is to explain how it affects the other two sides.
steps.example: A client wants their new website finished two weeks earlier (less time). To do this, the team either hires extra developers (more cost) or removes some features (less scope). One corner moved, so another had to move too.
keyPoints: A constraint is a limit that ties your hands.
The triple constraint is scope, time, and cost.
The three are connected like a triangle.
Change one side, and you usually must change another.

steps.title: Scope: what work is in and what is out
steps.body:
Scope is the first side of the triangle. The project scope is a document that defines the limits of the project. It says clearly what work will be done and what work will not be done.

The scope of work, often written as SOW, describes the deliverables, meaning the products or services the project will hand over at the end. A good scope helps everyone share the same understanding of what the project will deliver. A simple project may need only a short scope; a complex project needs a longer, more detailed one.

One big danger is scope creep. Scope creep happens when the work slowly grows bigger, little by little, without anyone noticing or managing it. Each small addition needs extra time and money that were not in the plan. If these changes are not tracked, the project can fail.

Changes to scope are normal. The problem is not change itself; it is unmanaged change. A good project manager uses a change management system to record every change and get it approved. The later a change comes, the more it usually costs.
steps.example: A team agrees to build a garden with a lawn and flowers. Then the client keeps adding 'small' extras: a path, then lights, then a pond. Nobody updates the budget or schedule. This is scope creep, and the project runs out of money.
keyPoints: Scope defines what work is inside and outside the project.
Deliverables are the products or services handed over at the end.
Scope creep is slow, unmanaged growth of the work.
Manage change with a system that records and approves each one.

steps.title: Quiz: understanding scope creep
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of scope.

quiz.question: What is 'scope creep' in a project?
options: When the project finishes earlier than planned
When the work slowly grows bigger without being tracked or approved
When the team writes a very detailed scope document
When the client pays more money than the budget
quiz.explanation: Scope creep is the slow, unmanaged growth of project work, which uses extra resources that were never in the plan.

steps.title: Time: building and managing the schedule
steps.body:
