To answer this, the chapter uses three tools: net present value (NPV), return on investment (ROI), and payback analysis. Each looks at costs and benefits in a different way. Together they help leaders see if the numbers work before the real work starts.
steps.example: For the campus store, the sponsor funds a feasibility study. If it shows the store would lose money every year, the project is terminated before any shelves are bought.
keyPoints: Feasibility asks if a project is possible and worth it.
The sponsor decides go or no-go.
Approving the study triggers the planning phase.
Money tools: NPV, ROI, and payback analysis.

steps.title: Net Present Value (NPV)
steps.body:
The first money tool is net present value, or NPV. It starts from one simple idea: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future. Money you have now can earn interest, and prices rise over time. So future money is worth less to us today.

NPV takes this into account. It looks at all the cash coming in and all the cash going out over the years. It then shrinks each future amount back to what it is worth today, using a discount rate. The discount rate reflects the return you could earn elsewhere. After shrinking every amount, you add them all up.

The result tells you the value the project adds. If NPV is above zero, the project would add value and may be accepted. If NPV is below zero, the project would subtract value and should be rejected. If NPV is exactly zero, it adds no money value, so you decide using other reasons, like strategy.
steps.example: A five-year project costs $75,000 up front. After discounting all costs and benefits at 12% and subtracting, the NPV is a positive $105,175. A positive NPV means the project may be accepted.
keyPoints: Money today is worth more than money later.
NPV discounts future cash back to today's value.
NPV above zero: may accept; below zero: reject.
NPV of zero: decide on other, non-money reasons.

steps.title: ROI and Payback Period
steps.body:
The second tool is return on investment, or ROI. ROI measures how efficient an investment is. It shows profit compared to the money you put in. To calculate it, you subtract the project's costs from its benefits, then divide by the costs.

A simple example: if you invest $100 and it becomes $110 next year, your gain is $10. Divide $10 by $100 and you get 0.1, which is a 10% return. In the chapter's larger example, the ROI comes out to about 52%, which is a strong result.

The third tool is payback analysis. It answers a different question: how long until the project earns back the money spent on it? This is the moment when the benefits start to be larger than the costs. The clearest way to see it is to chart the running totals of costs and benefits over time. Where the benefit line passes the cost line is your payback point.
steps.example: In the chapter's example, when you chart cumulative benefits and costs, the benefits grow larger than the costs in the second year. So the payback period is about two years.
keyPoints: ROI = (benefits minus costs) divided by costs.
ROI shows profit compared to money invested.
Payback period is the time to earn the money back.
Chart running totals to find the payback point.

steps.title: Quiz: Reading an NPV Result
steps.body: You have learned what NPV tells you. Let's test it.

quiz.question: A project's NPV is calculated as a positive number (above zero). What does this suggest?
options: The project would subtract value and should be rejected.
The project adds no value, so decide on other reasons.
The project would add value and may be accepted.
The project has no costs at all.
quiz.explanation: A positive NPV means the investment would add value to the organisation, so the project may be accepted.

steps.title: Build: A Mini Business Case
steps.body:
Time to practise. Pick any small project idea you like, such as a lunch delivery service for your office, a study-group app, or a small shop.

Write a short business case with these five parts, one or two sentences each:
1. Problem or opportunity: what need are you serving?
2. Two or three possible solutions.
3. Benefits and costs of your preferred solution.
4. One SMART objective (specific, measurable, time based).
5. One risk that could hurt the project.

Keep it simple and realistic. The goal is to practise thinking like a project manager, from need to preferred solution.
steps.example: Idea: a campus coffee cart. Opportunity: no coffee near the library. Solutions: cart, vending machine, or partner cafe. Preferred: cart, low cost, high foot traffic. SMART objective: "Serve coffee from the cart by 1 September, selling 50 cups a day within two weeks." Risk: bad weather lowers sales.

steps.title: The Project Charter
steps.body:
Once a project is approved, we write a project charter. It is also called a project definition or project statement. The charter is the document that officially starts the project and gives it authority.

The charter states the scope, the objectives, and the people in the project. It gives an early outline of roles and responsibilities. It names the main stakeholders. Very importantly, it defines the authority of the project manager, meaning what the manager is allowed to decide. The charter acts as a reference of authority for the whole life of the project.

The purpose of the charter is threefold. First, it helps everyone understand the project, why it is being done, and its justification. Second, it sets the general scope early, so people know the rough size and limits. Third, it names the project manager and their level of authority. The charter should also say who will review and approve it.

Think of the charter as the project's birth certificate. It makes the project official and gives the manager permission to lead.
steps.example: A charter might begin: Project Name: Rice University Computer Store Creation; Sponsor: Jane Ungam, Facilities Manager; Project Manager: Fred Rubens. These lines make the project and its leader official.
keyPoints: The charter officially starts the project.
It states scope, objectives, and key people.
It defines the project manager's authority.
It also says who reviews and approves it.

steps.title: Inside the Charter: Part 1
steps.body:
A project charter is built from clear sections. Let's look at the first ones.

The Identification section lists the basics: the project name, the date of this version, the sponsor's name and authority, the revision number, and the project manager's name. This tells readers exactly which project and version they are looking at.

The Overview gives a short, precise statement of the project. Just one or two sentences that say what the project is.

The Objective section states the goals clearly, with a measure of success. It should follow the SMART rule you learned. Write goals that are specific, measurable, and time based.

The Scope section defines the range of the work. It lists what the project will do. Just as importantly, it also lists what the project will not do. Saying what is out of scope prevents confusion later and stops the project from quietly growing bigger than planned.
steps.example: For the campus store, scope includes: decide what supplies to sell, set prices, find vendors. Out of scope: store design and construction. Naming both sides keeps everyone clear on the limits.
keyPoints: Identification lists name, date, sponsor, and manager.
Overview is a short, precise project statement.
Objective follows the SMART rule.
Scope says what is included and what is excluded.

steps.title: Inside the Charter: Part 2
steps.body:
The charter continues with more useful sections. Each one adds clarity.

Major Milestones are the key checkpoints needed to finish the project. Major Deliverables are the concrete results the project will produce, the actual things you hand over.

Assumptions are facts you are not fully sure of, but you state them so the project can move forward as if they were true. You can confirm them later. Constraints are limits on the project or the team. A constraint is anything that limits the range of solutions or approaches, such as a tight budget or a fixed deadline.

The charter also states the Business Need or benefits, explaining why the project was created and how it helps the organisation. It gives a preliminary cost statement, saying how cost will be defined and controlled. It lists Project Risks, where a risk is anything uncertain that could reduce the chance of success. Finally, it names the stakeholders and their roles.
steps.example: A charter risk for the store: "An election may change tax rules for university shops." An assumption: "Only computer supplies will be sold." A constraint: "No project planning software is available."
keyPoints: Milestones are checkpoints; deliverables are results handed over.
Assumptions are stated so the project can proceed.
Constraints are limits like budget or deadlines.
Risks are uncertain events that could reduce success.

steps.title: Teach Back: The Project Charter
steps.body:
Let's make sure the charter is clear in your mind. Imagine a new team member asks you, "What is a project charter, and why do we need it?"

In your own simple words, explain what a charter is and what job it does. Try to mention: that it officially starts the project, that it sets scope and objectives, and that it defines the project manager's authority. You could also explain why saying what is 'out of scope' is so useful. Keep it friendly and short.
steps.example: You might say: "A charter is like the project's birth certificate. It names the project, its goals, and its boundaries, and it says what I, as project manager, am allowed to decide. It makes the project official."

steps.title: The Statement of Work (SOW)
steps.body:
Closely linked to the charter is the statement of work, often shortened to SOW. While the charter gives the project authority and the big picture, the SOW focuses on describing the work itself in more detail.

A statement of work explains what work will be done, what the deliverables are, and the boundaries of that work. In plain words, it answers: what exactly are we going to do, and what will we hand over? It draws on the same ideas you saw in the charter's scope, objectives, milestones, and deliverables, but it puts the work front and centre.

A good SOW reduces misunderstandings, just like a clear objective does. Remember the painter and the "pleasing blue" office. When the work is written down clearly, both sides agree on what will be delivered before the work starts. This protects the customer and the team.

The SOW is especially useful when you hire an outside vendor, because it becomes the shared record of what was promised.
steps.example: A SOW for the campus store's supplier might state: source and deliver 200 memory sticks, 100 mouse pads, and cables by 15 August, delivered to the store, with agreed prices. Both sides sign and agree.
keyPoints: The SOW describes the work and its deliverables in detail.
It answers what will be done and what is handed over.
Clear work descriptions prevent misunderstandings.
It is vital when working with outside vendors.

steps.title: Getting Formal Approval
steps.body:
The last step of initiation is getting a clear, official yes. A project should not truly begin until the right people approve it.

Approval happens at a few points. First, the project sponsor approves the business case, and money is set aside for a feasibility study. The sponsor is the senior person who backs the project and controls its funding. Second, when the feasibility study is finished and approved, that triggers the planning phase. If the study is not approved, the project stops.

The project charter also needs formal acceptance. The charter includes a section for the names, titles, and signature lines of the people who will sign off on it. These signatures are the official permission to proceed. They show that the leaders agree on the project, its scope, and the manager's authority.

Why does this matter? Formal approval protects everyone. It confirms that the decision was made openly, that funding is agreed, and that the project has real support. With sign-off, the team can move into planning with confidence.
steps.example: The campus store charter has signature lines. When the sponsor and dean sign, the project is officially approved and the manager can begin planning the work.
keyPoints: The sponsor approves the business case and funds the study.
Approving the feasibility study triggers planning.
The charter is signed off by named leaders.
Sign-off gives official permission and support.

steps.title: Quiz: Who Approves and What Happens Next?
steps.body: You have reached the end. Let's check one key idea about approval and feasibility.

quiz.question: What happens when a feasibility study is completed and approved?
options: The project is immediately finished and closed.
It triggers the beginning of the planning phase.
The business case is written for the first time.
The project manager loses all authority.
quiz.explanation: Completing and approving the feasibility study triggers the planning phase; if it is not approved, the project stops instead.

title: Planning & Scope
intro: Every strong project starts with a good plan. In this module you will learn how project managers plan their work before they build anything. You will see what "scope" means, how to write it down clearly, and how to break big work into small pieces with a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). You will also learn how to stop your project from slowly growing out of control. The lessons use simple English and real examples from the textbook, like cleaning a room and making holiday eggnog. Take your time, and try the practice tasks. By the end, you will be able to plan the scope of a small project with confidence.

steps.title: What is project planning?
steps.body:
After a project is defined and the team is chosen, the next big step is planning. Planning sits at the heart of every project. It tells everyone where you are going and how you will get there.

In the planning phase, you write down your plans, list what the project will deliver, and build the schedule. These plans help you manage many things: time, cost, quality, changes, risk, and people. They also help you control your own team and any outside suppliers, so you finish on time and inside your budget.

Planning is often the hardest phase for a project manager. You must make smart guesses about the staff, tools, and money you will need. You may also plan how the team will talk to each other and how you will buy services from other companies.

Good planning has clear goals: set the business needs, set the cost and schedule, list what you will deliver and when, plan your resources, and get approval from management before you move on.
steps.example: Before building a house, you first make drawings, list materials, set a budget, and agree on a finish date. That planning work guides everything that comes next.
keyPoints: Planning tells everyone where the project is going and how to get there.
Plans help manage time, cost, quality, changes, risk, and people.
Planning is often the hardest phase because you must make smart guesses.

steps.title: The parts of a project plan
steps.body:
A project plan is not one document. It is a set of smaller plans that work together. Each one covers a different part of the project.

Here are the main planning steps:

- Scope planning: decide all the work that is inside the project.
- Work Breakdown Structure: break the project into tasks and smaller tasks.
- Schedule development: list all activities and put them in order.
- Resource planning: decide who does what work, and when, and what special skills are needed.
- Budget planning: work out how much the project will cost.
- Procurement planning: plan how you will buy services from outside companies.
- Risk management: plan for problems that could happen, and prepare backup plans.
- Quality planning: decide how you will check that the work is good.
- Communication planning: decide how you will keep everyone informed.

You do not need to memorise every item. The key idea is simple: a real plan covers many areas, not just the schedule. In this module we focus on the first parts, scope and the WBS, because they come first and shape everything else.
steps.example: Think of a wedding plan. It includes the guest list (scope), the day's timetable (schedule), the money (budget), the caterer contract (procurement), and a plan for bad weather (risk).
keyPoints: A plan is many smaller plans working together.
Scope planning and the WBS come first and shape the rest.
Other parts cover schedule, budget, resources, risk, quality, and communication.

steps.title: Turning objectives into SMART goals
steps.body:
The planning phase takes the project's objectives and makes them clear. An objective is simply a goal the project must reach. At the start, people often describe goals in vague, feeling-based words, like "we want a fast system." That is too soft to measure.

The project manager works with the user to turn soft words into hard numbers. Instead of "fast," you say "every screen must load in under three seconds." Now you can test it and know when it is done.

A helpful rule for writing objectives is SMART:

- Specific: written in clear, exact terms.
- Measurable: use numbers so you know when it is finished.
- Acceptable: agreed with the stakeholders.
- Realistic: possible to actually achieve.
- Time based: has a real deadline, an end date.

When you follow SMART, your goals can be measured. That means you can prove success instead of just hoping for it. Clear objectives also become the base for your requirements later.
steps.example: "Christmas Cheer, Inc. will produce two million cases of holiday eggnog, shipped to distributors by October 30, for $1.5 million or less." Everyone can measure if this goal is met.
keyPoints: An objective is a goal the project must reach.
Replace vague words with numbers you can measure.
SMART means Specific, Measurable, Acceptable, Realistic, and Time based.

steps.title: Teach it back: SMART goals
steps.body: Imagine a friend shows you this goal: "Make the website better." In your own simple words, explain why this goal is weak. Then rewrite it as a SMART goal. Say what each SMART letter adds, and give one example number and one deadline you would use.
steps.example: You might rewrite it as: "Reduce the checkout page load time to under two seconds by March 31." Explain which SMART parts this now meets.
keyPoints: Point out that "better" cannot be measured.
Add a number so success is clear.
Add a real end date, not just "soon."

steps.title: Quiz: SMART objectives
steps.body: Test your memory of the SMART rule.

quiz.question: Which objective best follows the SMART rule?
options: The app should feel modern and friendly.
The app should load the home screen in under 2 seconds by June 1.
The app should be as fast as possible.
The app should make users happy soon.
quiz.explanation: Only this one is specific, measurable (2 seconds), and time based (by June 1), so success can be proven.

steps.title: What is scope?
steps.body:
Before you start any work, you want to know exactly what work must be done. Scope is the answer to that question. Project scope is all the work needed to meet the project's objectives, and nothing more.

Scope planning is the very first thing you do to manage your project's work. The idea is simple but powerful. At the start, you build a clear picture of everything the project will do. As the project moves on, you keep that picture up to date and written down in a document called the scope management plan.

Why does this matter so much? If the work is not clear, team members will guess. Some may do too much, some too little, and some may do the wrong thing. A clear scope keeps everyone pointed at the same target.

Scope also has a second, quieter job: it says what is not included. Knowing what is out of scope is just as useful as knowing what is in. It protects your time, your budget, and your team.
steps.example: If you hire a painter, the scope might be "paint the two bedrooms." That clearly includes the bedrooms and clearly leaves out the kitchen and the garden fence.
keyPoints: Scope is all the work needed to meet the objectives, and nothing more.
Scope planning is the first step in managing your work.
A good scope also says clearly what is out of scope.

steps.title: Deliverables: what the project produces
steps.body:
Once your objectives are clear, you list your deliverables. A deliverable is anything your project produces. That includes the final product or service for the client, and also every item you make along the way.

Deliverables are not only the big final result. They include documents, plans, schedules, budgets, blueprints, and even the project management papers you create. Some are final deliverables, and some are intermediate, meaning they are steps on the road to the final result.

Every deliverable must be a real, measurable thing. You should be able to point to it and check it. Because of this, each deliverable must be described in enough detail so you can tell it apart from similar ones.

For example, there is a big difference between a twin engine plane and a single engine plane, or a daily report and a weekly report. If your description is too loose, people may build the wrong thing. Writing clear deliverables is one of the project manager's most important jobs.
steps.example: For the holiday eggnog project, one deliverable is the cartons that hold the eggnog. It is a real item you can see, count, and check.
keyPoints: A deliverable is anything the project produces.
They include final results and the smaller items made along the way.
Describe each one clearly so it cannot be confused with a similar item.

steps.title: Requirements: the details of each deliverable
steps.body:
After you list the deliverables, you write the requirements. A requirement describes what the final deliverable must have or must do. In short, a requirement is an objective that must be met. Requirements answer questions like who, what, where, when, and how much.

Requirements must be measurable and testable. There are six common types:

- Functional: what the deliverable should do, in plain words ("the system should store a customer's order").
- Non-functional: limits and rules on the deliverable ("the system is available 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.").
- Technical: how it will be built (which hardware, which language).
- Business: the reason the organization wants the project.
- User: what users need to do with the product.
- Regulatory: laws and licenses you must follow. These are usually non-negotiable.

You do not need to master all six now. Just remember that requirements turn a general deliverable into a clear, checkable list of features and limits. When you write them, also record why each decision was made. That way, if someone asks for a dropped feature again later, you can explain the reason and avoid repeating work.
steps.example: For a bank ATM: a functional requirement is "let the user choose to print a receipt." A regulatory requirement is "connect to an approved, uninterrupted power source."
keyPoints: A requirement is an objective the deliverable must meet.
Requirements must be measurable and testable.
Record why a decision was made, not just what was decided.

steps.title: Quiz: functional vs non-functional
steps.body: You buy trucks to move a load from a warehouse to a shop. Look at these statements.

quiz.question: Which statement is a NON-functional requirement (a limit or constraint)?
options: The truck should carry a load from the warehouse to the shop.
The load area must be at least 10 feet high.
The truck should be able to move goods.
The truck should deliver items to the store.
quiz.explanation: "At least 10 feet high" is a constraint that limits the choices, so it is non-functional; the others just say what the truck should do.

steps.title: Getting requirements: sign-off and traceability
steps.body:
Writing good requirements is one of the hardest jobs a project manager faces. Weak requirements almost always lead to weak results. So it is worth doing carefully.

You do not have to gather requirements alone. You bring in stakeholders, such as business analysts, process owners, customers, and team members. You can collect ideas through interviews, focus groups, brainstorming, prototypes, watching users work, and surveys. The project manager mostly guides and supports this process, rather than doing every part alone.

When the requirements are written down, ask the stakeholders to sign off on them. Sign-off means they agree that the document says what they truly want. This step is important because it creates a shared agreement everyone can point to later.

Many teams also use a requirements traceability matrix. This is a simple table that links each requirement to its origin and follows it through the whole project. It makes sure every requirement adds value, and it gives you a clear, organised way to manage changes to the scope.
steps.example: A traceability matrix row might say: requirement SYSADM 1.2, its source, its owner, its priority, its current status, and whether testing passed.
keyPoints: Weak requirements lead to poor project results.
Ask stakeholders to sign off so everyone agrees on what is wanted.
A traceability matrix links each requirement to its origin and tracks it.

steps.title: The scope statement
steps.body:
The scope statement is the document that ties your scope together. It lists the project's deliverables and describes the main objectives, and those objectives should include measurable success criteria.

In broad terms, the scope statement captures the product of the project. For example: "development of a software system to capture and track software orders." It also names who will use the product and what features it will have.

As a baseline, a scope statement usually contains:

- The project name and the project charter.
- The owner, sponsors, and stakeholders.
- The problem statement.
- The goals and objectives.
- The requirements and the deliverables.
- The non-goals, which are the things that are out of scope.
- Milestones and cost estimates.

The non-goals line is easy to skip, but it is one of the most valuable parts. By writing down what the project will not do, you set a clear edge around the work. Some organizations add more sections too, such as assumptions, risks, and acceptance criteria.
steps.example: A scope statement for a class website might list the goal, the pages to build, the launch date, and a clear non-goal: "We will not build an online payment system in this project."
keyPoints: The scope statement lists deliverables and describes the main objectives.
It should name what is out of scope (the non-goals).
It also includes owner, stakeholders, milestones, and cost estimates.

steps.title: Build: write a mini scope statement
steps.body:
Pick a small, real project you could do, such as organising a team lunch, building a simple blog, or planning a study group. Write a short scope statement with these five lines:

1. Project name.
2. One or two SMART objectives (with a number and a date).
3. Two or three deliverables.
4. One clear non-goal (something out of scope).
5. A rough cost estimate.

Keep it short, just a few sentences per line. The goal is to practise setting a clear edge around your work.
steps.example: Name: Team Lunch. Objective: host a lunch for 12 people by Friday for under $150. Deliverables: booking, menu, invites. Non-goal: no evening event. Cost: about $130.
keyPoints: Use SMART wording for the objectives.
Always include at least one non-goal.
Short and clear beats long and vague.

steps.title: The Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
steps.body:
Now that your deliverables and requirements are clear, you break the work into pieces. The tool for this is the Work Breakdown Structure, or WBS. The WBS defines the scope of the project and splits the work into parts that you can schedule, estimate, and control.

The idea is simple. You take one big, complicated task and divide it into smaller tasks. Then you divide those into even smaller tasks. You keep going until you reach a level that cannot be usefully split further. If you have ever organised folders and files on a computer, or drawn a family tree, you already know this shape.

Why break work down like this? Because small tasks are much easier to estimate. It is hard to guess the time and cost of a huge task, but easy to guess them for a tiny one. Each lower level of the WBS shows more detail about the work.

One important rule: when you build a WBS, you do not worry about the order of the tasks or which task depends on another. Order comes later, when you build the schedule. The WBS only captures all the work.
steps.example: To clean a room, the WBS might have branches like "pick up items," "vacuum," "dust furniture," and "clean curtains," with each branch split into smaller steps.
keyPoints: The WBS splits big work into smaller and smaller tasks.
Small tasks are far easier to estimate for time and cost.
Do not worry about task order in a WBS; that comes with the schedule.

steps.title: Work packages and the 100 percent rule
steps.body:
A WBS is a tree. It starts with the end goal at the top and breaks down into phases, deliverables, and tasks. Each item gets a unique number, like 3.0, 3.1, 3.2. These numbers help you track cost, schedule, and resources for each piece.

You can organise a WBS in different ways. You can group it by deliverable, for example a book, a CD, and a DVD for a multimedia project. Or you can group it by phase, where each phase is the first level. The project manager decides how many levels to use. Use enough levels to estimate time and cost well, but not so many that the pieces become hard to tell apart.

The lowest level of the WBS is called a work package. A work package is small enough to give to one person or one small team, with clear responsibility for finishing it. This is the level where you make your time, cost, and resource estimates.

The most important rule is the 100 percent rule. It says that all the child pieces under a parent must add up to 100 percent of the parent's work. Nothing is missing, and nothing extra is added.
steps.example: Under "3.0 Vacuum," the children might be get out the vacuum, connect the hose, vacuum the carpet, empty the bag, and put it away. Together they cover all of vacuuming.
keyPoints: A work package is the lowest level, small enough for one person or team.
Time, cost, and resource estimates are made at the work-package level.
The 100 percent rule: child pieces must add up to all of the parent's work.

steps.title: Teach it back: the WBS
steps.body: A classmate says: "A WBS is just a to-do list in the right order." In your own simple words, explain two ways this is wrong. Cover what a WBS really is, why we break work down, and why order does not belong in a WBS. Then explain the 100 percent rule using any everyday example.
steps.example: You could use making breakfast: break it into eggs, toast, and coffee, then explain that these three together must cover the whole breakfast (the 100 percent rule).
keyPoints: A WBS is a tree of work, not an ordered list.
We break work down so small tasks are easy to estimate.
The 100 percent rule means the parts fully cover the whole.

steps.title: Quiz: the work package
steps.body: Think about the lowest level of a WBS.

quiz.question: What is a 'work package' in a WBS?
options: The top-level goal of the whole project.
The order in which tasks must happen.
The lowest-level task, small enough for one person or team, where estimates are made.
The total budget for the project.
quiz.explanation: The work package is the lowest WBS level, small enough to assign clearly, and it is where time, cost, and resource estimates are set.

steps.title: Avoiding scope creep
steps.body:
Scope creep is when a project slowly grows bigger than planned. Small extra requests are added one by one, and each seems harmless. But together they can break your schedule, blow your budget, and tire out your team. Good scope planning is your best defence.

Mseveral tools from this module help you hold the line. First, a clear scope statement, with named non-goals, sets a firm edge around the work. When someone asks for extra work, you can point to that edge. Second, stakeholder sign-off gives you a shared agreement about what was truly promised.

Third, record the reasons behind decisions. If a feature was dropped because it was not feasible, write that down. If someone asks for it again later, you can explain why, instead of quietly redoing wasted work. Fourth, the traceability matrix gives you a structured way to manage changes to the scope, so changes are seen and controlled, not sneaked in.

Changes are not always bad. Sometimes a project truly needs to change. The point is to make changes on purpose, through a clear process, and not by accident.
steps.example: A website project agreed to build five pages. Later, stakeholders ask for a blog, a shop, and a forum. Because the scope statement named these as non-goals, the team can discuss them as real change requests, not silent extras.
keyPoints: Scope creep is slow, unplanned growth of the work.
A clear scope statement, sign-off, and recorded reasons help you resist it.
Changes should be made on purpose through a clear process, not by accident.

steps.title: Build: make a small WBS
steps.body:
Take the small project from your earlier scope statement, or pick a new everyday task like planning a birthday party. Build a small WBS on paper or on screen.

Steps:

1. Write the end goal at the top.
2. Break it into 3 or 4 main branches (deliverables or phases).
3. Break each branch into 2 or 3 smaller tasks.
4. Number each item, like 1.0, 1.1, 1.2.
5. Check the 100 percent rule: do the children fully cover their parent?

Remember, do not worry about task order. Just capture all the work.
steps.example: Birthday Party (1.0) could break into Food (1.1), Guests (1.2), and Venue (1.3). Food might split into buy cake, order pizza, and prepare drinks.
keyPoints: Start with the goal, then break down into branches and tasks.
Number each item so you can track it.
Check that children cover 100 percent of the parent.

steps.title: Putting it all together
steps.body:
You have now walked through the first and most important parts of project planning. Let's bring the pieces together.

Planning tells everyone where the project is going. You start by making objectives SMART, so goals are clear, measurable, and have deadlines. Then you define your scope, which is all the work needed to meet those objectives, and nothing more. Naming what is out of scope is just as valuable as naming what is in.

Inside the scope, you list deliverables, the real things the project produces, and requirements, the clear details each deliverable must meet. You write these into a scope statement and ask stakeholders to sign off. Next, you break the work down with a WBS, splitting big tasks into small work packages that are easy to estimate, and you check the 100 percent rule.

Finally, you protect your plan from scope creep by holding a firm edge, recording your decisions, and handling changes through a clear process.

Do these steps well and your project starts on solid ground. You will know what to build, who will build it, and how you will know when it is done. That is the true power of planning.
steps.example: A team that wrote SMART goals, a clear scope statement with non-goals, and a simple WBS could confidently say no to a surprise request, because their plan clearly showed what was and was not included.
keyPoints: SMART objectives lead into a clear scope.
Deliverables and requirements feed the scope statement and the WBS.
Sign-off and recorded decisions protect you from scope creep.

title: Scheduling
intro: Welcome to Scheduling. A schedule is a plan that shows what work happens, in what order, and by when. In this module you will follow a real story: a wedding planner named Sally has only a few months to plan Susan and Steve's June wedding. Step by step, you will learn how to turn a long list of tasks into a clear, workable timeline. You will define activities, put them in the right order, draw a network diagram, find the critical path, build a Gantt chart, set milestones, and keep the plan on track. The language is simple and every term is explained. Let's begin.

steps.title: What scheduling means
steps.body:
A project schedule is a plan for time. It shows what work must happen, in what order, and by what date. Without a schedule, a team just hopes things get done. With one, the team can see the whole path from start to finish.

To build a schedule, you follow four steps in order. First, you define the activities. This means you list every small piece of work that must be done. Second, you sequence them. This means you put the activities in the right order, because some work must wait for other work. Third, you estimate the resources. This means you decide who and what you need for each activity. Fourth, you estimate the time. This means you guess how long each activity will take.

When these four steps are done, you can turn the results into pictures and charts that are easy to read. This module walks through each step using a real wedding as the example. Take it slowly. Each idea builds on the one before it.
steps.example: Sally the wedding planner starts by asking a simple question: what work needs to be done before June? Her answer becomes the first draft of the schedule.
keyPoints: A schedule shows what work happens, in what order, and by when
Building it has four steps: define, sequence, estimate resources, estimate time
Each step feeds the next one

steps.title: Defining the activities
steps.body:
Defining activities means breaking the work into small, doable pieces. In an earlier stage, a project team builds a Work Breakdown Structure, or WBS. The WBS groups the work into larger blocks called work packages. Now you take each work package and split it into the single tasks needed to finish it.

An important point: activities are not the same as deliverables. A deliverable is the finished result, like the printed invitations. An activity is one unit of work you do to get there, like "choose the paper" or "print the cards." You want each activity small enough that you can estimate its time and cost.

Two tools help you here. The first is expert judgment. This means asking people who have done similar work before. They can help you list tasks you might forget. The second is rolling-wave planning. This means you plan in detail only the part you understand well now. For the parts you do not know yet, you leave a placeholder, and you fill in the details later when you know more.
steps.example: Sally lists tasks like "find caterer," "shop for dress," and "mail the invitations." Each is one small unit of work, not the whole wedding.
keyPoints: Activities are small units of work that build a deliverable
A deliverable is the finished result; an activity is a step toward it
Expert judgment and rolling-wave planning help you list activities

steps.title: The wedding: a real case
steps.body:
Meet the example that runs through this module. Susan and Steve want to get married in June, but it is already January. There is so much to plan: invitations, flowers, a cake, a dinner menu, and a band. They feel overwhelmed, so they hire a wedding planner named Sally.

Sally is calm. She has planned many events, and she knows the right tools will help her put every task in order. Her first job is to figure out what work needs to be done. She writes a simple to-do list, then arranges those items into a Work Breakdown Structure so the big blocks of work are clear.

Next she writes the activity list. This is a full list of everything that must be done to complete the project, including every activity inside each work package. For each activity, Sally also writes activity attributes. Attributes are the notes about that activity: a short description, what must come before it, what comes after it, and any limits on time or resources. These notes are where the real planning starts, because they tell Sally the order of the work.
steps.example: Susan notes a real order problem: "We can't do the seating arrangements until we have the RSVPs." That waiting rule is exactly the kind of note stored in the activity attributes.
keyPoints: The activity list names everything that must be done
Activity attributes are the notes for each activity
Attributes include the description and the order of the work

steps.title: How activities depend on each other
steps.body:
Most activities are connected. One task often must wait for another. The task that comes first is called the predecessor. The task that follows is called the successor. There are four ways these can link.

The most common link is finish-to-start (FS). It means the first task must finish before the second can start. You cannot mail the invitations until you have printed them. When people say one task "depends on" another, this is usually what they mean.

The second link is start-to-start (SS). Two tasks begin at the same time. The third link is finish-to-finish (FF). Two tasks end at the same time. The fourth link is start-to-finish (SF), which is rare. Here one task must start before another can finish. For example, the musicians cannot stop playing until the guests have started to leave.

Why does this matter? Because these links decide the order of your whole schedule. If you get the links wrong, the plan will not work in real life.
steps.example: Printing invitations and addressing them have a finish-to-start link: the printing must finish before the addressing can begin.
keyPoints: Predecessor comes first; successor follows
Finish-to-start (FS) is the most common link
The other links are start-to-start, finish-to-finish, and start-to-finish

steps.title: Quick check: predecessors
steps.body: Let's test the most common link between tasks.

quiz.question: In a finish-to-start (FS) relationship, what must happen?
options: Both tasks must start at the same moment
The first task must finish before the second can start
Both tasks must finish at the same moment
The second task must finish before the first can start
quiz.explanation: Finish-to-start means the predecessor's finish leads into the successor's start, like printing invitations before mailing them.

steps.title: Three kinds of predecessors
steps.body:
Not all "must come first" rules are the same. The chapter names three kinds, and knowing the difference helps you decide which ones you can change and which you cannot.

A mandatory predecessor exists because of the nature of the work itself. You truly cannot do the second task first. You cannot address an invitation that has not been printed yet. This kind of rule is fixed. You cannot argue with it.

A discretionary predecessor is a choice, not a must. It is based on preference or on past experience of what works well. Susan and Steve want the bridesmaids to arrive at the reception before the couple arrives. Nothing forces this order; they simply prefer it. Because it is a choice, you could change it if needed.

An external predecessor comes from outside your project. Your work waits on someone or something you do not control. Sally cannot decorate the reception hall until the earlier wedding party has left it. That other party is outside her project, so it is an external dependency.
steps.example: "Print, then address" is mandatory. "Bridesmaids arrive first" is discretionary. "Wait for the other party to leave the hall" is external.
keyPoints: Mandatory: forced by the nature of the work
Discretionary: a preference you could change
External: depends on something outside your project

steps.title: Teach it back: predecessor types
steps.body: In your own simple words, explain the difference between a mandatory, a discretionary, and an external predecessor. Give one wedding example for each. Then say why it helps a planner to know which type a rule is. Try to answer before reading on.

steps.title: Leads and lags: adjusting the timing
steps.body:
Sometimes tasks should not follow each other back-to-back. You may want to add or remove time between them. Two ideas handle this: lag and lead.

Lag time is a delay you add on purpose between a predecessor and its successor. You wait a while before starting the next task. For example, when the bride dances with her father, the other guests wait a short time before they join the dance. That pause is a lag.

Lead time is the opposite. You let the successor start early, before the predecessor is fully finished. You overlap the two tasks a little. For example, you might have the caterer start preparing dessert an hour before everyone finishes eating dinner. The dessert work begins early so it is ready on time.

Leads and lags give your schedule flexibility. They let you fit real life, where clean back-to-back timing is not always best. Use a lag when a gap is helpful. Use a lead when an early start saves time.
steps.example: A lag: guests wait before joining the father-daughter dance. A lead: the caterer starts dessert an hour before dinner ends.
keyPoints: Lag adds a delay between two tasks
Lead lets the next task start early and overlap
Both add flexibility to the schedule

steps.title: Quick check: lead vs lag
steps.body: Let's make sure lead and lag are clear.

quiz.question: The caterer starts preparing dessert one hour before dinner is finished. This overlap is an example of:
options: Lag time
Lead time
A milestone
A mandatory predecessor
quiz.explanation: Lead time lets a successor task begin before its predecessor is done, so the two tasks overlap.

steps.title: Milestones: your checkpoints
steps.body:
A milestone is an important checkpoint in a project. It is not work itself; it is a marker that shows a key point was reached. Milestones have no length of time. They are moments, like "invitations sent" or "location booked."

Why use them? Milestones let everyone quickly see progress. Some milestones may be listed in a contract as things you must reach to succeed. Others are just important points you want to watch. Your milestone list should make clear which milestones are required and which are simply helpful to track.

For Susan and Steve's wedding, good milestones might be: invitations sent, menu finalized, location booked, and bridesmaids' dresses fitted. Each one marks that a major piece of work is complete.

One more point. As you plan the activities, you may find the project needs to change. When that happens, you do not just change it quietly. You create a change request and send it through the change control system, so the right people approve the change. This keeps the plan honest and agreed by all.
steps.example: Steve and Susan asked that invitations be printed at least three months early so guests have time to reply. "Invitations sent" becomes a milestone on Sally's list.
keyPoints: A milestone is a checkpoint, not work, and has no duration
Some milestones are required; some are just useful to track
Scope changes go through a change request and change control

steps.title: Sequencing: putting work in order
steps.body:
Once you know all the activities, you decide the order. This is the activity sequencing process. It answers the question: what comes first, second, third, and so on?

Sally already did most of the hard thinking when she wrote the activity attributes. Remember, those notes list the predecessors and successors for each task. So sequencing is where she uses those notes to line up the whole flow of work. She also checks her milestone list, which holds the major pieces of work, and any approved scope changes she found along the way.

A key idea in sequencing is that not everything must be done one after another. Some tasks can happen at the same time, side by side. This is called working in parallel. Other tasks must wait for input from an earlier task. Good sequencing finds both: what can run in parallel to save time, and what must wait.

When you finish sequencing, you have the raw order of the project. Next you will turn that order into pictures that are easy to read and share.
steps.example: Sally realized she needed a second limo for the bridesmaids. She sent that as a change request through change control, got approval, and added it to the plan.
keyPoints: Sequencing puts activities in the right order
It uses the predecessors and successors from the attributes
Some tasks run in parallel; others must wait

steps.title: The Gantt chart
steps.body:
A Gantt chart is a bar chart that shows a project schedule. It was created by Henry Gantt. It is popular because it is easy to read. Time runs across the top. Each task is a horizontal bar. The length of the bar shows how long the task takes, and its position shows when it starts and ends.

The tasks are placed in order. The chart starts in the top-left corner and moves down to the bottom-right corner. This makes the flow of work easy to follow. A Gantt chart can be drawn quickly, so it is often the first tool a project manager uses to get a rough idea of how long the project will take.

Gantt charts can hold extra information too. You can mark milestones with a diamond or triangle, meetings with a circle, and progress reviews with a square. This turns one chart into a simple control panel for the whole project.

The chart also shows spare time. If a task has room to move without delaying the project, that spare time is called slack or float. It shows where the schedule has flexibility.
steps.example: In a directory project, key stage K could start as early as week 17 but is not needed until week 23. Those extra weeks of freedom are called slack.
keyPoints: A Gantt chart shows tasks as bars along a timeline
It is quick to draw and easy to read
Slack or float is spare time a task can move without delaying the project

steps.title: Quick check: the Gantt chart
steps.body: Let's confirm one key idea from the Gantt chart.

quiz.question: On a Gantt chart, what does "slack" (or "float") tell you?
options: A task is already late
A task can move or wait without delaying the whole project
A task must start immediately
Two tasks must finish together
quiz.explanation: Slack or float is spare time; it shows flexibility, where a task can shift without pushing back the project end date.

steps.title: Teach it back: the Gantt chart
steps.body: Imagine a friend has never seen a Gantt chart. In a few simple sentences, explain what it looks like, what the bars mean, and why a project manager likes to use it early. Add one sentence about what slack or float shows. Try it in your own words first.

steps.title: The network diagram
steps.body:
A network diagram is a picture that shows how tasks relate to each other. Where a Gantt chart focuses on time along a bar, a network diagram focuses on the links between tasks. It shows which task feeds into which.

The tasks in the diagram come from the WBS. Every task must be included. If you leave even one task out, your total time, cost, and resource plan could all be wrong. So the network must be complete.

There are two common styles. The first is activity-on-node (AON), also called the precedence diagramming method. Here each task sits in a box, and arrows between the boxes show the order. AON is the most common style, and every project management software program supports it. The second style is activity-on-arrow (AOA). Here the arrows are the tasks and circles are the connection points. The famous PERT chart is this style.

AON is used more often because it is simpler and it can show leads and lags. AOA is more limited: it can only show finish-to-start links, and it sometimes needs "dummy" activities just to show a dependency.
steps.example: Sally draws the invitation tasks as boxes joined by arrows to show how each step depends on the one before. That box-and-arrow picture is an AON network diagram.
keyPoints: A network diagram shows how tasks link together
Every WBS task must be included, or the plan may be wrong
AON (boxes) is more common and flexible than AOA (arrows)

steps.title: The critical path
steps.body:
The critical path is the longest path of tasks through your network diagram. That sounds strange, so let's be careful. Because some tasks must wait for others, there are several possible routes from start to finish. The route that takes the most time is the critical path.

Why the longest? Because the project cannot finish until even its slowest chain of tasks is done. So the critical path tells you the shortest possible time to complete the whole project. You add up the estimated times of all the tasks on that path, and that sum is your earliest finish date.

Here is the most important rule. Every task on the critical path must finish on time. If even one of them is late, the whole project is late. These tasks have no slack. That is why the critical path shows you exactly which activities to watch most closely.

If the critical path is longer than the sponsor wanted, you must renegotiate the timeline. The critical path does not just measure time; it tells you where your attention matters most.
steps.example: In the directory project the critical path is A–B–C–D–E–F–I–L, and adding those stages gives 28 weeks. That is the earliest the project can finish.
keyPoints: The critical path is the longest route through the network
It equals the shortest possible time to finish the project
Tasks on it have no slack, so any delay delays the whole project

steps.title: Quick check: the critical path
steps.body: Let's confirm the big idea of the critical path.

quiz.question: Why must every task on the critical path finish on time?
options: Because they are the cheapest tasks
Because they have slack to spare
Because any delay on this path delays the whole project
Because they are the only tasks with milestones
quiz.explanation: The critical path has no slack, so if one of its tasks is late, the whole project's finish date slips.

steps.title: Build a mini schedule
steps.body:
Time to try it yourself with a small project: baking and decorating a birthday cake. Do these four things on paper or in a note.

1) Define at least five activities (for example: buy ingredients, bake the cake, let it cool, make the icing, decorate the cake).

2) Sequence them. For each activity, write its predecessor. Mark at least one finish-to-start link (for example, the cake must finish baking before you can decorate it).

3) Find any tasks that can run in parallel. For example, you could make the icing while the cake is cooling. Note them.

4) Estimate a time in minutes for each activity, then trace the longest path from start to finish and add up the times. That longest path is your critical path, and its total is the shortest time to finish the cake.

As a bonus, add one milestone (for example, "cake fully cooled") and note where you might use a lead (start the icing before the cake is fully cool) or a lag (wait ten minutes before serving). This small exercise uses every idea from the module.

title: Resource Planning
intro: Every project needs the right people and things in the right place at the right time. This module teaches you resource planning. You will learn what a resource is, how to guess how much you need, and how to give clear roles with a RACI chart. You will also learn resource levelling, which fixes days when people have too much work. Finally, you will see how to build a strong, trusting team. The lessons use a simple wedding story, so the ideas stay easy and real.

steps.title: What Do We Mean by Resources?
steps.body:
A resource is anything a project needs to get the work done. Many people think only of workers. But resources are more than people.

Resources are people, equipment, a place, money, or anything else you need to finish the activities you planned. Look at your activity list. Every single activity needs resources. A cake needs a baker, an oven, and money. Music needs a band and a sound system.

Before you give resources to your project, you must know their availability. Availability means three things: what you can use, when you can use it, and any special rules. Some resources must be booked early. A hall or a special expert may only be free on certain days.

If a resource is hard to get on the day you need it, that is a resource constraint. A constraint is a limit that you must work around. Knowing your constraints early saves you from a bad surprise later. Once you understand your resources and their limits, you are ready to estimate how many you need.
steps.example: In the wedding story, a June wedding is harder to plan than a December one. The wedding halls are all booked far in advance for June. That limited hall time is a clear resource constraint.
keyPoints: A resource is any person, tool, place, money, or item a project needs.
Every activity on your list needs resources assigned to it.
Availability = what you can use, when, and under what rules.
A resource constraint is a limit you must plan around early.

steps.title: Estimating the Resources You Need
steps.body:
Estimating resources means deciding what each activity needs before the work starts. The chapter gives five simple tools to help you guess well.

First, expert judgment. You ask people who have done this work before. Their experience saves you from big mistakes. Second, alternative analysis. You look at more than one way to do a task. Maybe two workers for one day, or one worker for two days. You compare the options and pick the best.

Third, published estimating data. Other people share numbers from past projects in books, articles, and journals. You use their data as a guide. Fourth, project management software, like Microsoft Project. The program helps you work out needs and find good matches.

Fifth, bottom-up estimating. You break a big activity into small pieces. You estimate each small piece. Then you add all the pieces together for the total. This method is very accurate, but it takes a lot of time because you must check every small part.
steps.example: For catering, you break the job into small parts: choose a menu, order food, cook, and serve. You estimate the people and hours for each part, then add them up. That is bottom-up estimating.
keyPoints: Expert judgment: ask people who have done the work before.
Alternative analysis: compare different ways to do a task.
Published data and software give you numbers and help.
Bottom-up estimating is accurate but slow; you add small parts into a total.

steps.title: Duration and Effort Are Not the Same
steps.body:
After you know the resources, you can guess how long each activity will take. This is called activity duration estimating. You look at each activity, its size, and its resources, then you estimate the time.

Most of the time you start with a rough guess. Then you make it better and more exact. Small jobs may take a few hours. Big jobs may take days.

Here is a key idea. Duration and effort are different. Duration is the amount of time an activity takes, from start to finish. Effort is the total number of person-hours that people spend on it.

An example makes this clear. Say two people carve an ice sculpture. They work together for six hours. The duration is six hours, because that is how long it lasted. But the effort is twelve person-hours, because two people each worked six hours. Two times six equals twelve. Knowing both numbers helps you plan the schedule and the cost. Duration tells you time on the calendar. Effort tells you how much human work you must pay for.
steps.example: Two people carve an ice sculpture together in six hours. The duration is six hours. The effort is twelve person-hours (2 people x 6 hours). Same task, two different numbers.
keyPoints: Duration is how long an activity takes from start to finish.
Effort is the total person-hours all workers spend on it.
Start with a rough estimate, then make it more exact.
Duration shapes the schedule; effort shapes the cost.

steps.title: Quick Check: Estimating Tools
steps.body: Let's check the estimating ideas from the last lessons.

quiz.question: Which technique means breaking a big activity into small pieces, estimating each piece, then adding them up?
options: Expert judgment
Bottom-up estimating
Alternative analysis
Published estimating data
quiz.explanation: Bottom-up estimating breaks work into small parts, estimates each, and adds them into a total; it is accurate but takes time.

steps.title: Managing People: The Most Important Resource
steps.body:
Resource management means using an organization's resources well, and having them ready when they are needed. These resources include money, stock, human skills, and information technology.

Of all the resources, people are the most important. A project needs the right skills at the right moment. The person you need in week two may be different from the person you need in week eight, because the work changes as the project moves along.

People are also tricky to plan. One person may work on more than one project at the same time. Sometimes a worker is borrowed from their normal job because they have a special skill the project needs. And sometimes you cannot find the skill inside your own organization. Then you must hire outside help through a contract or an outside company.

So human resource planning is about getting the right people and timing them well. You match the skill to the phase, and you make sure people are free when the schedule needs them. Good people planning, done early, keeps the whole project on track.
steps.example: A company may run several projects in one year. One employee could work on two of them at once. If a rare skill is missing inside the company, the project manager hires an outside expert on contract for just that part.
keyPoints: Resource management = using resources well and having them ready on time.
People are the most important project resource.
Different project phases need different skills.
When a skill is missing inside, you hire outside help by contract.

steps.title: Clear Roles Stop Confusion
steps.body:
A project runs on many people doing many tasks. If people are not sure who does what, work slows down or gets missed. The chapter is clear: role confusion is a sign of weak organization skill. So giving clear roles is a core job for the project manager.

Roles and responsibilities answer simple but vital questions. Who does this task? Who checks it? Who must be asked first? Who just needs to hear the result? When everyone knows the answer, the team moves fast and trusts each other.

The project manager also decides how much power to hand out. This is called delegation. Delegation means giving work and decision power to other people. If the manager keeps all decisions, the project waits and slows down. If the manager gives power to people who lack the skill, mistakes happen. The skill is finding the right balance.

Good managers are good delegators. They match each task to the person with the right knowledge and skill. Clear roles and smart delegation together keep the project calm and moving.
steps.example: On a project, the procurement leader may also report to the company's vice-president for buying. But for day-to-day work, they take direction from the project manager. The manager decides how much freedom that leader gets.
keyPoints: Role confusion is a sign of weak organizational skill.
Clear roles answer: who does it, who checks it, who is asked, who is told.
Delegation means giving work and decision power to others.
The skill is balance: too little delegation slows work; too much causes mistakes.

steps.title: The RACI Responsibility Matrix
steps.body:
A great tool for clear roles is the RACI matrix. A matrix is just a simple chart. RACI is a way to write down who does what for each task, so nobody is confused.

RACI stands for four roles. R is Responsible: the person who actually does the task. A is Accountable: the one person who owns the result and answers for it. C is Consulted: people you ask for advice before you act. I is Informed: people who only need to hear the outcome.

You build the chart like a table. You list tasks down the left side. You list people across the top. In each box, you write R, A, C, or I. Now every person can look at the chart and see their part.

One golden rule: each task should have only one A. If two people are Accountable, they may both think the other one owns it, and the task falls through the gap. Many people can be Responsible or Consulted, but only one is Accountable. RACI turns messy roles into a clear, shared picture.
steps.example: For 'book the caterer': Susan is Responsible (she does it), Steve is Accountable (he owns the result), the wedding planner is Consulted (asked for advice), and Aunt Jane is Informed (just told the choice).
keyPoints: RACI is a chart of who does what for each task.
R = Responsible (does it), A = Accountable (owns it).
C = Consulted (asked first), I = Informed (told the result).
Golden rule: exactly one 'A' per task, or work falls through the gap.

steps.title: Teach It Back: RACI
steps.body: Now teach RACI to someone else in your own simple words. Imagine a friend has never heard of it. Explain what each letter means: R, A, C, and I. Then explain the golden rule about how many people can be Accountable for one task, and why that rule matters. Use one small example of your own, like planning a birthday party or a school event.
steps.example: You might say: 'R does the work, A owns it, C gives advice, I just hears about it. Only one person can be A, so nobody can pass the blame.'

steps.title: Quick Check: RACI Roles
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of the RACI chart.

quiz.question: In a RACI matrix, how many people should normally be 'Accountable' (A) for one task?
options: Exactly one person
As many as possible
Nobody at all
At least three people
quiz.explanation: Only one person should be Accountable per task; with two, each may assume the other owns it and the task can fail.

steps.title: When People Have Too Much Work
steps.body:
Sometimes a plan asks one person, or one machine, to be in two places at once. That is called an over-allocation. It means you have given a resource more work than it can do at that time.

To fix this, project managers use resource levelling. Levelling looks at how resources are used over time. It finds the days that are too busy and the days that are too empty. Then it smooths them out. The goal is to reduce both shortages and waste.

How does levelling work? When two tasks need the same person at the same time, you cannot do both together. So you move one task to a later time. Now the tasks run one after the other, not at the same moment. The over-allocation is gone.

Levelling can also balance the whole workload across the project, so no week is crazy busy while another is quiet. Perfect use of every resource, 100 percent of the time, is almost impossible. But smoothing the peaks and filling the gaps makes the plan realistic and kinder to your team.
steps.example: Imagine the same DJ is booked to set up sound at two events in the same hour. That is an over-allocation. Levelling moves one setup to a later time so the DJ can do both, one after the other.
keyPoints: Over-allocation = a resource is given more work than it can do at once.
Resource levelling smooths busy and quiet periods over time.
To fix a clash, you move a task so tasks run one after another.
Levelling reduces both shortages and wasted resources.

steps.title: Levelling and the Critical Path
steps.body:
Resource levelling is helpful, but it has one important cost you must watch. When you move tasks to a later time, the whole project can finish later too.

Remember the critical path. It is the longest chain of tasks through the project. Every task on it must finish on time, or the whole project is late. A delay in any critical-path task delays the end date.

So here is the danger. If levelling delays a task that sits on the critical path, the project's finish date moves back. The team may be less stressed, but the deadline slips. That is a real trade-off you must decide about with open eyes.

If the delayed task is not on the critical path, you may have some free time, called slack. Then moving it may cause no harm at all. This is why knowing your critical path is so useful. It tells you which tasks you can safely shift, and which ones you must protect. Levelling is powerful, but always check the critical path before you move a task.
steps.example: You move a task to balance a busy worker. But that task is on the critical path. Result: the wedding is now ready a week later than planned. The workload is smoother, but the deadline moved.
keyPoints: The critical path is the longest chain of tasks; a delay there delays the whole project.
Levelling a critical-path task can push the finish date later.
Tasks with slack (spare time) can often be moved with no harm.
Always check the critical path before you shift a task.

steps.title: Quick Check: Resource Levelling
steps.body: Let's check what levelling can do to your schedule.

quiz.question: What can happen if resource levelling delays a task that is on the critical path?
options: The project finish date can become later
Nothing changes at all
The project always finishes earlier
The budget disappears
quiz.explanation: Any delay to a critical-path task delays the whole project, so levelling such a task can push the finish date later.

steps.title: Managing and Growing the Team
steps.body:
A project succeeds through its team. The project manager has a big job: help each team member give their best, and help them grow as people at the same time. Each person should feel free to share ideas and work with others toward one shared goal.

To do this well, the manager uses performance evaluation. This means checking how the work is going and how each person is doing. It gives the manager the facts needed to keep the team skilled, positive, and accountable.

When the manager reviews a person's work, they look at three things. First, the quality and quantity of what the person produces. Second, work behavior, like being on time. Third, job attributes, like cooperation and taking the first step.

After a review, a good manager takes action. They tell the person what they did well. They also point out weak areas kindly and clearly. They fix problems with anyone who is falling below what is expected. And they reward strong performers, so those people keep doing excellent work. Feedback that is honest and fair helps the whole team get stronger.
steps.example: A manager notices a team member is always on time and cooperates well, but their reports have errors. In the review, she praises the punctuality and cooperation, then gives clear, kind help to improve the report quality.
keyPoints: The manager helps each member perform well and grow.
Performance review checks output quality, behavior, and attributes.
Give feedback on strengths and on areas to improve.
Reward strong performers and help those falling behind.

steps.title: Teach It Back: Resource Levelling
steps.body: Explain resource levelling to a classmate in your own words. Cover three things. First, what an over-allocation is. Second, how levelling fixes it (what you do with the clashing tasks). Third, the one big warning about the critical path. Keep it simple and use a short example of your own.
steps.example: You might say: 'If one person is booked for two jobs at once, that is over-allocation. We move one job to later so they run one after the other. But if that job is on the critical path, the whole project ends later.'

steps.title: Trust Builds Strong Teams
steps.body:
Skills alone do not make a great team. Trust does. Trust is the foundation for every relationship on a project. Without a basic level of trust, communication breaks down. Then costs rise and the schedule slips.

Think of trust as a filter. Information passes between people through this filter. When trust is high, information flows easily and freely. When trust drops, the filter gets thick, and important news gets stuck. A project that needs lots of open sharing will suffer badly without trust.

Building trust starts with the project manager. The manager sets an example of honesty and shows that lying has a high cost. The manager also makes sure the stated rules and the real, enforced rules match. When words and actions agree, people believe what they hear.

Time for informal talk matters too. At the start of a project, let team members get to know each other as people, not just workers. A short coffee chat helps a person feel understood. From that human connection, real trust can grow. A trusting team shares problems early, while they are still small and easy to fix.
steps.example: On one project, a team member gave the client wrong information and hid the mistake. The manager removed that person. This showed the team that honesty was expected and lying had a high cost, and the client's trust returned.
keyPoints: Trust is the foundation of every project relationship.
Trust is a filter: high trust lets information flow; low trust blocks it.
Managers build trust by matching stated rules with enforced rules.
Informal talk at start-up helps personal trust grow.

steps.title: Build a Simple RACI Chart
steps.body:
Time to practice. Pick a small project you know, like planning a class party, a trip, or a birthday. Write down four tasks for it. Then list three or four people (real or made up).

Make a RACI chart. Put the tasks in a column on the left and the people across the top. For each task, give exactly one A (Accountable), at least one R (Responsible), and add C (Consulted) or I (Informed) where they fit. Check that no task has two A's.
steps.example: Task 'order the cake': Maria = A (owns it), Tom = R (buys it), Dad = C (asked about budget), Grandma = I (just told the flavor). Repeat this for three more tasks.

steps.title: Choosing the Right Type of Team
steps.body:
Not every problem needs a team. Sometimes one person is faster and cheaper. A single person is best when speed matters, when that person already has the skills and resources, when the work is very detailed, or when a document must be written. Writing is really a solo task.

A team is better in other cases: when no single person knows enough to solve the problem, when many people must agree to the solution, when the problem crosses different project areas, or when you need real innovation.

When you do form a team, pick the right kind. A functional team works inside one area, like the engineering team or the procurement team. It works well when the job is clear and not too complex.

A cross-functional team mixes people from two or more areas. They bring different skills to one shared challenge. A problem-solving team is formed to fix one specific issue that appears during the project. Once the problem is solved, this team breaks up. Matching the team type to the job is a key project management skill.
steps.example: A cross-functional team in Tennessee mixed script writers, equipment buyers, and project controls. Because they talked often, they saved three weeks and several thousand dollars on an educational video project.
keyPoints: Use one person for speed, detail, or writing a document.
Use a team when knowledge, buy-in, or innovation is needed.
Functional teams work inside one area; cross-functional teams mix areas.
Problem-solving teams fix one issue, then disband.

steps.title: Quick Check: Duration vs Effort
steps.body: One more check on a key difference from earlier.

quiz.question: What is the difference between duration and effort?
options: Duration is total person-hours; effort is calendar time
Duration is the time an activity takes; effort is the total person-hours worked
They mean exactly the same thing
Effort can only be measured in days
quiz.explanation: Duration is how long the activity lasts on the calendar; effort is the total person-hours all workers spend on it.

steps.title: Plan Resources for One Activity
steps.body:
Now plan resources like a real project manager. Choose one activity from a small project, for example 'cook and serve food for 20 guests.'

Use bottom-up estimating. Break the activity into three or four small steps. For each step, write the people you need, the equipment or items, and a rough time. Then add the times to get a total duration. Also work out the effort: if two people work three hours, that is six person-hours. Finally, note one resource constraint you might face, such as an oven that is only free in the morning.
steps.example: Cook and serve food: (1) buy ingredients, 1 person, 2 hours; (2) cook, 2 people, 3 hours = 6 person-hours; (3) serve, 3 people, 2 hours. Constraint: the rented kitchen is only free before noon.

steps.title: Putting Resource Planning Together
steps.body:
Let's bring it all together. Resource planning makes sure the right people and things are ready at the right time.

You start by knowing your resources and their availability. You watch for constraints, like a hall that books up early. Then you estimate what each activity needs, using tools like expert judgment, alternative analysis, published data, software, and bottom-up estimating. You remember that duration and effort are different numbers.

Next, you plan people with care, because people are the most important resource. You give clear roles, and a RACI chart makes this easy: one Accountable person per task, with Responsible, Consulted, and Informed roles filled in. You delegate with balance, not too little and not too much.

When the plan asks too much of one person, you use resource levelling to smooth the load. But you always check the critical path first, because delaying a critical task delays the whole project.

Finally, you build the team. You review performance, give kind and honest feedback, choose the right team type, and grow trust. Skills matter, but trust is the foundation. With good resource planning, your project stays realistic, calm, and on track. Well done for finishing this module.
steps.example: Steve and Susan felt overwhelmed at first. By listing resources, estimating needs, setting clear roles, levelling the load, and building trust, their messy wedding plan became a schedule they could actually follow.
keyPoints: Know your resources, their availability, and constraints first.
Estimate needs; remember duration and effort differ.
Use RACI for clear roles and delegate with balance.
Level the workload carefully, and build a team on trust.

title: Budget Planning
intro: Money decides what a project can do. In this module you will learn how to estimate costs, sort them into types, and build a real budget. You will meet the cost baseline, and two kinds of safety money: contingency and management reserves. Finally, you will learn to control costs while the work runs, using simple earned value numbers. We follow John's apartment move from the textbook as a friendly, real example the whole way through. Short lessons, clear words, and quick practice will make budgeting feel doable.

steps.title: Why every project needs a budget
steps.body:
Every project comes down to money. If you have a bigger budget, you can hire more people, work faster, and deliver more. That is why no project plan is finished until you have a budget. This is true for small projects and big projects. The way you find the final number is always the same.
