First, you make careful cost estimates for everything in the project. An estimate is your best guess of how much something will cost. Then you add all those estimates together into one budget plan. After that, you can track your real spending against the plan while the work is happening.

Often, before you even start, people already expect a certain cost or time. Your job is to turn that hope into real numbers you can trust. Good numbers keep everyone calm and honest.

In this module, you will learn how to estimate costs, sort costs into types, build a budget, protect it with reserve money, and control it while the work runs. Take it one step at a time. By the end, budgeting will feel much less scary.
steps.example: A team hopes a new website 'should cost about $10,000.' The project manager turns that hope into a detailed budget by estimating each task, then tracks real spending against it.
keyPoints: No project plan is complete without a budget
Estimate each cost, then add them into one plan
Track real spending against the plan as work happens

steps.title: The first rough estimate
steps.body:
Early in a project, you do not know much yet. So your first cost guess is rough. This early guess is called a rough order-of-magnitude estimate. Some people call it a ballpark estimate. It just means a wide, quick guess, not an exact number.

This rough estimate is normal and useful. It helps people decide if the project is worth doing at all. As time goes on and you learn more, the estimate becomes sharper and more exact. Early estimates are fast and use few resources. They often lean on the expert judgment of experienced managers, who can guess well even with little information.

Estimating also matters when a company must choose between projects. To compare projects fairly, you need a cost estimate for each one. The estimate must be accurate enough for the comparison to mean something. But do not spend more time on the estimate than the project size deserves. A small project needs only a small, quick estimate.

Remember: a rough estimate is a starting point, not a promise. You will improve it as you go.
steps.example: Before a company picks between building an app or a website, a manager gives each a ballpark cost so leaders can compare them and choose.
keyPoints: A rough order-of-magnitude (ballpark) estimate is a wide early guess
It gets more exact as you learn more about the project
Match the effort of estimating to the size of the project

steps.title: Tools for estimating cost
steps.body:
When you estimate costs, you have several helpful tools. Here are four common ones from the chapter.

First, determination of resource cost rates. Every person who works on the project has a pay rate, like dollars per hour. Every material, like wood or wiring, also has a rate. This tool means finding the rate for labor and materials so you can do the math.

Second, vendor bid analysis. Sometimes you hire an outside company, called a vendor or contractor, to do part of the work. You may ask several vendors to bid, meaning they each send a price. This tool is about comparing those bids and choosing the best one.

Third, reserve analysis. You set aside extra money in case something expensive goes wrong. If your project has known risks, it is smart to keep some cash ready.

Fourth, cost of quality. Doing the project right costs money too. Checking work and fixing bugs early is cheaper than fixing them late. Cost of quality tracks the money spent on these quality activities.

After using these tools, you reach a full cost estimate. Always keep your notes and assumptions.
steps.example: For an office remodel, the manager finds the electrician's hourly rate, compares three flooring vendor bids, sets aside cash for surprises, and budgets for inspections.
keyPoints: Resource cost rates: the price of labor and materials
Vendor bid analysis: compare outside bids and pick one
Reserve analysis: set aside cash for possible overruns
Cost of quality: the money to do the work right

steps.title: Check: early estimates
steps.body: Let's check your understanding of early estimating.

quiz.question: What is a rough order-of-magnitude (ballpark) estimate?
options: A wide, quick early guess that gets more exact over time
A final, exact price the team must never change
A legal contract with a vendor
The money set aside only for emergencies
quiz.explanation: A ballpark estimate is a wide early guess made with little information, and it becomes more refined as the project develops.

steps.title: Analogous estimate
steps.body:
One fast way to estimate is to look at a similar past project. This is called an analogous estimate. The word 'analogous' just means 'similar.' If a similar project cost a certain amount, you can assume your project will cost about the same.

Of course, few projects are exactly alike. So you adjust the number up or down to fit the differences. If your project is bigger or harder, you raise the estimate. If it is smaller or simpler, you lower it. Choosing which past project to copy, and how much to adjust, depends on the estimator's judgment. Experienced managers do this well because they have made many estimates, including wrong ones they learned from.

Less-experienced managers can still use this method. They read the records from past projects. Some companies rate project complexity with a tool called the Darnall-Preston Complexity Index, or DPCI. It gives each project a profile. A manager can find past projects with a similar profile, then compare their first estimates with their final real costs. This gives a helpful reference and saves years of trial and error.

Analogous estimates are quick, but they are only as good as the match.
steps.example: John plans to move apartments. A friend says his similar move last year cost about $575 for the truck, boxes, and gas. John estimates his move will cost under $700.
keyPoints: An analogous estimate copies the cost of a similar past project
Adjust the number up or down for real differences
It relies on good records and the estimator's judgment

steps.title: Parametric estimate
steps.body:
Another method uses simple math with measurable factors. It is called a parametric estimate. A parameter is a measurable factor you can put into an equation to get a result. Size and location are examples of parameters.

Here is the idea. For work that is common across many projects, average costs per unit are known. So you multiply a measured amount by a cost-per-unit value. That gives your estimate quickly and fairly accurately.

Think about building a standard office building. The estimator asks two things: how many square feet, and which city. From size and location, they predict the cost. They already know the average cost per square foot and how local labor changes it. Other parameters, like the quality of finishes, refine the number further.

So the formula is simple: measured parameter multiplied by cost per unit. Parametric estimates work best when your project is made of standard, repeatable parts with known unit prices. When the work is unusual, this method is less reliable, and you may need a more detailed approach.

Parametric estimates are still guesses, but they use real data, so they can be trusted more than a pure hunch.
steps.example: A truck rental company sizes John's truck by the number of bedrooms. John has a one-bedroom apartment, so he picks the 14-foot truck; distance and days then set the price.
keyPoints: A parameter is a measurable factor, like size or location
Parametric estimate = measured amount x cost per unit
It works best for standard, repeatable work with known unit costs

steps.title: Teach it back: two estimate methods
steps.body: Time to teach, not just read. Imagine a coworker who has never estimated a project cost. In your own simple words, explain the difference between an analogous estimate and a parametric estimate. Say what each one is based on, and give one short example of each. Then say when you would trust the parametric method more. Keep it friendly and clear, as if you were helping a friend.
steps.example: You might start: 'An analogous estimate copies a similar past project, like guessing a move costs about the same as a friend's move. A parametric estimate uses math, like cost per square foot times the size...'

steps.title: Bottom-up estimating
steps.body:
The most accurate method is also the slowest. It is called bottom-up estimating. Instead of guessing the whole project at once, you find the cost of every small item in every activity, including labor and materials. Then you add them all up.

Picture your project schedule as a pyramid. At the top are big, general tasks. As you go down, the tasks become smaller and more detailed. In bottom-up estimating, you price each item at the lowest, most detailed level. Then you add those small numbers to get the cost of the bigger levels above them.

Because you check every small piece, this method is usually more accurate than an analogous or parametric estimate. The trade-off is time. It takes real effort to list and price everything.

Software makes this easier. You can 'roll up' the numbers, which means subtotal them to show less detail when you want a summary. For simple projects, a spreadsheet works fine. For big projects, scheduling software can manage costs too and group them by activity or category.

When you truly need an accurate number, bottom-up is the method to choose.
steps.example: John lists every packing item, quantity, and price, plus the truck rental, on company websites. Adding it all up, his detailed bottom-up estimate comes to $661.25.
keyPoints: Bottom-up estimating prices every small item, then adds them up
It is the most accurate but the most time-consuming method
'Rolling up' means subtotaling detail into a summary

steps.title: Check: matching the method
steps.body: Let's match a situation to the right estimating method.

quiz.question: A manager prices every single item and activity in the schedule, then adds them all together. Which method is this?
options: Analogous estimate
Parametric estimate
Bottom-up estimating
Ballpark estimate
quiz.explanation: Bottom-up estimating finds the cost of each detailed item and sums them, making it the most accurate but slowest method.

steps.title: From estimates to a budget
steps.body:
Once you have your cost estimates, you turn them into a real budget. The idea is simple. You break the project into activities. Then you estimate the cost of each activity and add them together to get the total project cost.

This process of subtotaling costs by category or by activity is called cost aggregation. 'Aggregation' just means gathering many small parts into one total. You might group costs by type, like labor and materials, or by activity, like 'packing' and 'truck rental.' Both views are useful.

An activity can also have costs from several sources at once. It may include internal labor, internal materials, and payments to outside vendors. You can add an activity code to each detailed cost. Then you sort and subtotal by that code to see the full cost of each activity.

Why do this? Because a clear, organized budget is easy to track later. When you can see spending by activity and by category, you quickly notice where money is going. A messy budget hides problems. A well-organized one shows them early, while you still have time to act.

Good structure now saves stress later.
steps.example: John groups his detailed costs into two categories, packing materials and truck expenses, then subtotals each. Together they form the organized budget for his move.
keyPoints: Estimate each activity, then total them for the project cost
Cost aggregation means subtotaling costs by category or activity
Organized budgets make later tracking much easier

steps.title: The cost baseline and budget timeline
steps.body:
Your budget is not just one big number. It is spread across time. Every activity has a start date and a length, so you can calculate how much money will be spent by any date in the project. This time-based plan of spending becomes your baseline, the plan you will measure real results against.

Money is usually moved into the project account shortly before it is needed. This timing matters a lot. If money arrives too late, an activity is delayed because there is no cash to pay for it. If money arrives too early, the company loses the chance to use that cash elsewhere, or it pays extra interest on borrowed money.

So managers create a schedule of money transfers that matches the schedule of payments. The process of matching transfers to payments is called reconciliation. Done well, the cash is there exactly when each activity needs it, with little waste.

Be careful: sometimes there is no margin for error. If early activities cost more than the money available at that point, the project runs short of cash even if the total budget looks fine. Watching the timeline, not just the total, keeps you safe.
steps.example: A project transfers funds four times. For most activities there is more cash available than spent, but at activity 6 all the available money is used, leaving no cushion.
keyPoints: The baseline spreads the budget across the project timeline
Reconciliation matches money transfers to payment dates
Watch cash timing, not just the total, to avoid shortfalls

steps.title: Build: a small bottom-up estimate
steps.body:
Now you try it. Pick a small, real project you could imagine, like painting a room, hosting a birthday party, or setting up a home office. Make a simple bottom-up estimate.

List at least five items or tasks. For each one, write a quantity and a price. Then add them up to get a subtotal. Try to group your items into two categories (for example, 'materials' and 'services') and give each category its own subtotal. Finally, write the grand total.

Keep it short and realistic. The goal is to practice building a number from small parts, exactly like the bottom-up method.
steps.example: Painting a room: paint (2 cans x $30 = $60), brushes ($15), tape ($8) under 'materials' = $83; painter (4 hrs x $25 = $100) under 'services' = $100. Grand total: $183.

steps.title: Contingency reserves
steps.body:
Almost every project has a surprise that pushes costs above the first estimate. You cannot know exactly which activity will cost more, but it is safe to assume some of them will. Interestingly, if your estimates are almost never exceeded, they may be too high, and you should review your method.

So instead of padding every single cost, you set aside one pool of extra money for surprises. This money is called a contingency reserve. It covers unplanned but statistically predictable cost increases. That phrase means: you cannot predict the exact problem, but experience shows problems of some kind will appear.

Here is a key point. Because you expect this money will likely be spent, the contingency reserve is part of the total project budget. It is included in your baseline. If the reserve is big enough to cover the surprises, the project still finishes within budget. That is the goal.

Think of it like packing a little extra money for a road trip. You do not know if you will need a repair or a detour, but you keep some cash ready so a small problem does not stop the whole trip.

Contingency reserves keep a normal surprise from becoming a crisis.
steps.example: On John's move, small surprises are expected. Money set aside in a contingency fund covers these unplanned costs, so the move still finishes within budget.
keyPoints: Contingency reserve is one pool of cash for expected-but-unknown overruns
It is likely to be spent, so it is part of the budget baseline
If it is large enough, the project still finishes on budget

steps.title: Management reserves
steps.body:
There is a second kind of reserve, and it is different. Sometimes something happens that changes the scope of the project. Scope means the work the project is supposed to do. A scope change can be a challenge, or it can be a great opportunity.

For example, imagine a new technology appears that would make your finished project much better. Adding it costs more money and changes the scope. But it might be worth it. The problem is timing. You may need money right away, before you can officially negotiate a scope change with the sponsor or client.

For moments like this, money is kept in a management reserve. The manager can release it to meet needs that would change the project's scope. It gives you the freedom to act fast on a big opportunity or a serious problem.

Here is the key difference from contingency reserves. A management reserve is not likely to be spent. So it is not part of the project's budget baseline. It can still be included in the total project budget, but it sits outside the baseline as a special extra.

One reserve handles normal surprises; the other handles big, scope-changing events.
steps.example: During John's move, a chance appears that would change the scope of the project. Management reserve money could be released quickly, before a formal scope change is negotiated.
keyPoints: Management reserve covers events that change the project scope
It is not likely to be spent, so it is NOT in the budget baseline
It gives the manager fast money for big opportunities or problems

steps.title: Teach it back: two kinds of reserve
steps.body: Teach this one back. A new team member is confused about reserve money. In your own simple words, explain the difference between a contingency reserve and a management reserve. Be sure to cover two things: what each one is for, and whether each one is part of the budget baseline. Give one short example of when a manager would use each. Keep your explanation short, warm, and easy to follow.
steps.example: You might say: 'A contingency reserve is for small expected surprises and it IS in the baseline because we will probably spend it. A management reserve is for big scope changes, is not likely to be spent, and sits outside the baseline...'

steps.title: Check: reserves
steps.body: Let's check the two kinds of reserve money.

quiz.question: Which statement is TRUE about a management reserve?
options: It is used for small, expected cost overruns on normal activities
It is always fully spent on every project
It is part of the project's cost baseline
It is not part of the baseline and is used for scope-changing needs
quiz.explanation: A management reserve handles scope-changing needs, is not likely to be spent, and is not part of the budget baseline (unlike a contingency reserve).

steps.title: Controlling costs while work runs
steps.body:
Projects rarely go exactly as planned. So the project manager must watch spending and catch problems early. Controlling costs means comparing real spending to the plan, again and again, and reporting it to managers and stakeholders.

Here is a common mistake. Imagine John says, 'The project is going well. I am halfway through the days but have spent only a fifth of my budget.' That sounds great, but it is not a good report. Why? He compared money spent to the total budget. He should compare money spent to the amount that was planned to be spent by this point for the work that should be done by now.

To do this well, project managers use earned value management, or EVM. EVM regularly compares planned cost with actual cost during the project. It uses three key numbers.

Planned value (PV) is the budgeted cost of the work that should be done by a certain date. Earned value (EV) is the budgeted cost of the work that has actually been done. Actual cost (AC) is the real money spent on that work. These three numbers, PV, EV, and AC, let you see the true health of your budget and schedule.
steps.example: On day six of John's move, the plan called for spending $261.65 (PV). The work actually done was worth $162.10 (EV), and the real money spent was $154.50 (AC).
keyPoints: Controlling costs means comparing real spending to the plan, often
Compare spending to the planned amount for the work due by now
EVM uses three numbers: planned value, earned value, actual cost

steps.title: Variances and performance indexes
steps.body:
Once you have PV, EV, and AC, you can measure how the project is really doing. A variance is the difference between planned and actual progress.

Schedule variance (SV) tells you if you are behind or ahead of schedule. The formula is SV = EV minus PV. If EV is smaller than PV, SV is negative, which means less work was done than planned, so the project is behind schedule.

Cost variance (CV) tells you if you are over or under budget. The formula is CV = EV minus AC. A positive CV means the project is under budget, which is good news.

Variances give dollar amounts, but they do not show the size compared to the whole budget. For that, use ratios called indexes. The schedule performance index (SPI) is EV divided by PV. An SPI below 1 means behind schedule. The cost performance index (CPI) is EV divided by AC. A CPI above 1 means under budget.

Together these numbers tell a clear story. You can be under budget but behind schedule at the same time. Reading both keeps you honest about where the project truly stands.
steps.example: For John on day six: SV = $162.10 - $261.65 = -$99.55 (behind schedule). CV = $162.10 - $154.50 = $7.60. SPI = 0.62 and CPI = 1.05, so he is under budget but behind schedule.
keyPoints: SV = EV - PV; negative means behind schedule
CV = EV - AC; positive means under budget
SPI = EV/PV and CPI = EV/AC; below 1 is bad, above 1 is good

steps.title: Build: calculate a variance
steps.body:
Now practice the math yourself. Use these numbers from a small project on a given day: planned value (PV) = $500, earned value (EV) = $450, actual cost (AC) = $400.

Calculate four things and write a short sentence for each: (1) schedule variance, SV = EV - PV; (2) cost variance, CV = EV - AC; (3) schedule performance index, SPI = EV / PV; (4) cost performance index, CPI = EV / AC. For each result, say whether it is good or bad news for the project. Take your time and check your arithmetic.
steps.example: SV = 450 - 500 = -$50 (behind schedule). CV = 450 - 400 = $50 (under budget). SPI = 450/500 = 0.90 (behind). CPI = 450/400 = 1.13 (under budget). So: under budget, but a little behind schedule.

steps.title: Check: reading the numbers
steps.body: One last check on earned value numbers.

quiz.question: A project has a cost performance index (CPI) of 1.05. What does this tell you?
options: The project is under budget
The project is over budget
The project is exactly on budget
The project is behind schedule
quiz.explanation: CPI = EV/AC, and a value greater than 1 means the earned value is higher than the actual cost, so the project is under budget.

title: Procurement
intro: Welcome! In this module you will learn about procurement. Procurement is the work of buying goods and services from outside companies for your project. You cannot always do everything yourself, so you hire other people to help. We will follow the whole path: plan what you need, choose the right kind of contract, find and pick a seller, and then manage the work until it is done. You will meet three main contract types (fixed price, cost reimbursable, and time and materials) and learn when to use each one. Take your time, and enjoy the journey!

steps.title: What is procurement?
steps.body:
Procurement means buying goods and services from outside your team. When your project needs work that you cannot do yourself, you hire another company to do it. That company is called a seller, contractor, or vendor. Procurement management is the job of planning and controlling all this buying.

Procurement follows a clear, step-by-step order. First, you plan what you need to buy or hire out. Next, you plan how you will do it. Then you send your needs to sellers. The sellers bid, which means they offer a price to win the work. You pick the best one and sign a contract with them.

After the work starts, you watch it closely. You check that the seller follows the contract. When the work is finished, you close the contract and finish all the paperwork.

Think of procurement like hiring a builder for your house. You plan, you ask for prices, you choose, you sign, you watch the work, and you pay when it is done well. Every step matters, and skipping one can cause problems later.
steps.example: A software project needs a training course made. The team cannot build it, so they hire an outside company. They plan the need, ask sellers for prices, pick one, sign a contract, and check the work.
keyPoints: Procurement means buying goods or services from outside your team
It follows a clear order: plan, ask sellers, pick, sign, watch, close
The outside company is called a seller, contractor, or vendor

steps.title: The procurement management plan
steps.body:
Before you buy anything, you make a plan. First, you think about all the work you will hire out for your project. You check if hiring is really needed. Then you decide what kinds of contracts fit your project best.

The procurement management plan is a document that shows how the buying process will be run. It holds useful details, such as the types of contracts you will use and how you will measure the seller's performance. A metric is a number or measure you use to judge if the work is good.

The plan also lists the delivery dates for the work, the standard company documents you will use, and how many vendors will take part. It shows how buying affects your project's limits and your schedule. It notes lead times, which is the waiting time before a product arrives.

This plan is a smaller part of the bigger project management plan. Two useful tools during planning are make-or-buy analysis and choosing the contract type. We will learn both of these next.
steps.example: A plan might say: use fixed-price contracts, measure sellers on on-time delivery, use three vendors, and order long-lead machines early so they arrive before they are needed.
keyPoints: The procurement plan shows how the whole buying process will be run
A metric is a measure used to judge if the seller's work is good
The procurement plan is part of the larger project management plan

steps.title: Make-or-buy analysis
steps.body:
Make-or-buy analysis is a simple but important question. Should you do the work yourself (make), or should you hire another company (buy)? It can also mean: should you build a solution, or buy one that already exists?

To answer, you look at the same things you use for other big project choices. How much does it cost to build it yourself compared to buying it? How will the choice change your project scope, which is the amount of work in your project? How will it change your schedule? Do you have the time and people to do the work and still keep your promises?

Some resources, like heavy machines, can be bought, rented, or leased. Leasing means paying to use something for a set time, a bit like renting. You should compare the cost of leasing versus buying and pick the best path.

The main idea is simple: think carefully before you decide. Do not hire out work just because it feels easy, and do not keep work in-house just out of habit. Weigh the cost, time, and skills each time.
steps.example: A company needs a crane for two months. Buying costs a lot and they may not use it again. Renting it for two months is cheaper, so they choose to rent instead of buy.
keyPoints: Make-or-buy asks: do the work yourself, or hire someone else?
Weigh cost, scope, schedule, and your available time and skills
Heavy equipment can be bought, rented, or leased; compare the costs

steps.title: Quick check: make-or-buy
steps.body: Let's check the idea of make-or-buy analysis.

quiz.question: What is the main question a make-or-buy analysis tries to answer?
options: Which seller offers the lowest price?
Should we do the work ourselves or hire another company?
How many people should be on the project team?
When should the final report be written?
quiz.explanation: Make-or-buy analysis decides whether to do the work in-house (make) or hire an outside company (buy).

steps.title: Contract types: the big picture
steps.body:
A contract is a legal agreement between you (the client) and a seller. You should know the main kinds of contracts so you can pick the one that is fair and works well for both sides. There are three big types.

The first is fixed price. Here the client always pays the same amount, no matter how much time or effort the work takes. The price is set at the start and does not move. If the seller works harder than planned, their profit goes down, but your cost stays the same.

The second is cost reimbursable, also called cost plus. Here the seller charges you for the real cost of the work, plus a fee or rate on top. If the work needs more effort, your cost goes up, but the seller's fee is safe.

The third is time and materials. Here you pay a rate for the time the seller spends, and you also pay for all the materials they use. As the work grows, your cost goes up, and so does the seller's profit.

Each type shares risk in a different way. Risk means the chance of extra cost or trouble. Choosing the right type helps protect your project.
steps.example: Painting a room: fixed price means you pay $500 no matter what. Cost plus means you pay the paint cost plus a fee. Time and materials means you pay per hour plus the paint.
keyPoints: The three main types are fixed price, cost reimbursable, and time and materials
Fixed price: the client always pays the same set amount
Each type shares the risk of extra cost differently

steps.title: Fixed-price contracts
steps.body:
A fixed-price contract is a legal deal to provide goods or services at an agreed price. The contract usually spells out the quality needed, the timing, and the price. The client knows the cost in advance, which makes budgeting easy.

This type works best when the scope of work is very clear and not likely to change. Scope means the exact work to be done. With a fixed price, the seller carries the job of managing the work to meet the price. Your project team just tracks quality and schedule to make sure the seller delivers.

But there is a risk: change. If the project changes and the seller must do something new, the price for that change is usually very high. Because most of the cost risk sits with the seller, they often add a contingency. A contingency is extra money set aside to cover surprises. This makes fixed-price changes cost more than in other contract types.

To use a fixed price well, you need at least two good sellers to choose from, and a scope of work that will not change much. A clear scope and a clear contract are the keys to success.
steps.example: A print shop agrees to print 1,000 brochures for $2,000. The design is final and will not change. The client pays $2,000, and the shop manages the printing to stay on budget.
keyPoints: Fixed price gives the client a predictable, known cost
It works best when the scope of work is clear and stable
Changes are costly because the seller carries most of the cost risk

steps.title: Kinds of fixed-price contracts
steps.body:
Fixed-price contracts come in a few useful forms. Knowing them helps you pick the right fit.

A fixed-total-cost contract puts all costs, including profit, into one agreed price. The seller takes the risk if labor or materials cost more than expected.

A fixed-price contract with price adjustment is used for very long projects that run for years. The most common form adjusts for inflation, which is when prices slowly rise over time. In some countries money can lose value fast, so the contract price can change based on an inflation index. It can also adjust for prices that jump around, like the price of oil.

A fixed-price contract with incentive fee rewards the seller for doing better than the agreed plan. For example, they may earn extra for finishing early at an important milestone. A milestone is a key point or date in the project. These contracts often also have a penalty clause. This means the seller pays money if the work is late.

A fixed-unit-price contract sets a fixed price for each unit, like each hour or each item, when the total number needed is not yet known. Your team must estimate how many units will be used. If the guess is low, the project will cost more than the budget.
steps.example: A software deal adds an incentive: the seller earns a bonus if the app is ready before training starts. It also has a penalty: they lose money for each day the app is late.
keyPoints: Price-adjustment contracts handle inflation on very long projects
Incentive fees reward early or strong performance; penalties punish delay
A fixed-unit-price sets a price per unit when the total amount is unknown

steps.title: Teach it back: fixed price
steps.body: Now it is your turn to teach. Imagine a friend is new to project management. In your own simple words, explain what a fixed-price contract is, why the client likes it, and what the main risk is. Try to give one small example, like a paint job or a print job. Teaching an idea out loud is a great way to make it stick in your memory.

steps.title: Cost-reimbursable contracts
steps.body:
In a cost-reimbursable contract, you agree to pay the seller for the real cost of doing the work. This type is also called cost plus, because the seller gets their costs plus an extra fee.

This contract is used when the scope of work or the cost is not well known. When no one is sure how much the job will cost, this type helps. Because the client pays the real costs, the seller carries much less risk from rising costs. So sellers do not need to add a big safety amount to their bid.

But there is a downside. Because the seller gets paid back for costs, they have less reason to save money. Unless the contract gives a reward for saving, the seller may not try hard to cut costs.

This type needs good records. Every cost must be written down. Good documentation makes sure the seller is paid for all real work, and makes sure you do not pay for work that was never done. Keeping clear receipts and time records protects both sides.
steps.example: A research project has an unknown scope. The client agrees to pay all real lab costs plus a fee. The lab keeps careful receipts so both sides agree on what was spent.
keyPoints: Cost reimbursable (cost plus) pays the real cost plus an extra fee
It is used when the scope or cost is not well known
It needs good records, and the seller has less reason to save money

steps.title: Ways to pay the extra fee
steps.body:
In a cost-reimbursable contract, the seller is paid back for costs plus an extra amount. There are several ways to set that extra amount.

With a fixed fee, the seller's profit is a set amount agreed at the start. It does not change, even if costs go up or down.

With a percentage fee, the seller gets their costs plus a percentage of those costs, such as 5% of the total. If costs rise, the fee rises too.

With an incentive fee, the contract tries to push the seller to do well in areas that matter. Often it rewards the seller for saving or cutting project costs. This is a good way to make the seller care about lower costs.

With an award fee, the seller gets all allowed costs plus a fee based on how well they perform. The goals are often softer and based on judgment. Money is set aside, and the project team decides how much to give for excellent work.

Each method changes how the seller behaves. A percentage fee can reward higher spending, while an incentive fee rewards saving. Choose the method that matches your goals.
steps.example: A team wants the seller to save money, so they use an incentive fee: the more the seller lowers the cost while meeting goals, the bigger the reward they earn.
keyPoints: Fixed fee is a set profit that does not change
Percentage fee rises when costs rise; incentive fee rewards saving
Award fee is based on the team's judgment of excellent work

steps.title: Time and materials contracts
steps.body:
A time and materials contract, often called T&M, is used for small jobs that have high uncertainty. Uncertainty means you are not sure how much work or cost the job will need.

In a T&M contract, the seller charges an hourly rate for their time, plus the cost of the materials they use, plus a small percentage on top. The seller sends time sheets and receipts to show the hours worked and the items bought. You pay for the real time and the real materials.

Because the job is small and unclear, the project, not the seller, carries the risk. In other contract types the seller adds extra money to cover risk. In a T&M contract they do not need to, so the total cost to the project is often lower for these small, risky jobs.

To stay safe, the contract usually includes a not-to-exceed amount. This is a top limit; the seller cannot charge more than this agreed number. T&M lets you adjust as you learn more. You will not know the final cost until you have enough information to make a good estimate.
steps.example: A plumber fixes an odd leak. No one knows how long it will take. You agree to pay per hour plus parts, but set a not-to-exceed limit of $600 so the bill cannot grow forever.
keyPoints: T&M pays an hourly rate plus real material costs, for small unclear jobs
The project carries the risk, so cost is often lower for these jobs
A not-to-exceed amount sets a top limit the seller cannot pass

steps.title: Quick check: contract types
steps.body: Let's compare the three contract types.

quiz.question: In which contract does the client always pay the same set amount, no matter how much effort the work takes?
options: Cost-reimbursable (cost plus)
Time and materials
Fixed price
Award fee
quiz.explanation: In a fixed-price contract the price is set at the start and stays the same regardless of the effort applied.

steps.title: Progress payments and change management
steps.body:
Sellers usually need to be paid during the work, not only at the end. On long contracts, the seller spends a lot of money along the way and wants some of it back early. So the contract sets up a schedule of payments tied to finished work or milestones.

These early payments, based on how much work is done, are called progress payments. For each payment there is a set amount of work to finish, a time frame, and a quality standard the work must reach before the seller is paid.

Just like your project has a scope of work, each seller also has a scope of work. It says exactly what they will produce or supply. Often, changes happen during a project that change the seller's scope. The contract should say how these changes will be handled.

When a change is needed, you create a change order, which is a written change to the contract. Capturing changes early and writing down what changed keeps the project moving. If changes are not written down, team members may argue. A clear change process reduces conflict and protects the project.
steps.example: A course is built in three parts: design, development, and final delivery. The seller gets one progress payment after each part is finished and accepted, so three payments in total.
keyPoints: Progress payments pay the seller during the work, based on finished parts
Each payment needs set work, a time frame, and a quality standard
A change order is a written change to the contract that avoids conflict

steps.title: Teach it back: choosing a contract
steps.body: Time to teach again. A friend has a small, unclear repair job and does not know which contract to use. In your own simple words, explain why a time and materials contract might fit better than a fixed-price contract for a small, risky job. Mention the not-to-exceed limit and who carries the risk. Explaining your choice out loud helps you remember when to use each type.

steps.title: The procurement process and plan
steps.body:
The procurement process is the full cycle of buying. It runs from the moment you decide to buy something, all the way to paying the bills and closing the contracts.

After you decide to buy goods or hire out a service, the procurement team makes a plan. The plan picks the right relationship and contract type for each purchase. It prepares requests to sellers, evaluates the offers, awards and signs contracts, manages quality and timing, handles changes, and closes the contracts at the end.

Two common requests are the RFQ and the RFP. An RFQ is a request for quote, which mostly asks for a price. An RFP is a request for proposal, which asks for a full plan, not just a price. We will look at both more closely soon.

Each of these steps can take hours or even weeks, depending on how complex the project is. Every step goes into the project master schedule. Buying can affect your timeline, so long waiting times must be planned. Any buying step that could delay the project needs special care.
steps.example: For a new building, the team schedules each buying step. Ordering custom steel takes months, so they place that order early to avoid delaying the whole project.
keyPoints: The procurement process runs from the buy decision to closing the contract
The plan covers requests, evaluation, awarding, managing, and closing
Buying steps go on the schedule because they can delay the project

steps.title: Selecting the contract approach and risk
steps.body:
How do you pick the right approach? First, the technical team writes a description of the work to be hired out. Then the project management team asks a set of questions about it.

They ask: Is the work a common item, a custom product, or a unique skill? What kind of relationship is needed: a supplier, a vendor, or a partner? How should the seller be approached: by RFQ, RFP, or a personal contact? How well do we know the scope of work? What are the risks, and who should carry them? And how important is it to know the cost in advance?

The first three questions help decide how to reach and get the goods. The other questions help decide which contract type fits best.

A key factor is deciding who takes the most risk. The team chooses which risks the project will manage and which risks pass to the seller. Usually the project team wants to manage its own risk. But sometimes the seller has more skill or control, so they can manage a risk better. In that case, it is smart to pass that risk to them.
steps.example: A job needs a rare skill the team lacks. The seller understands it far better, so the team lets the seller carry the technical risk, because the seller can control it best.
keyPoints: Answer key questions to choose how to buy and which contract fits
The relationship may be a supplier, vendor, or partner
Decide who takes each risk; give a risk to the party who can control it best

steps.title: Asking for bids: RFQ and RFP
steps.body:
A solicitation is the act of asking sellers for a price and supporting information. It usually takes the form of an RFQ or an RFP. Partnerships are set up differently, case by case, by senior managers.

Before you ask, you find bidders. Bidders are people or companies able to supply the materials or do the work. On small projects, the parent company often has a list of trusted suppliers from past work. On unique projects with no list, the team builds a new list and qualifies the sellers, which means checking they are good enough to bid.

A seller is judged on two things: the ability to do the work well, and financial stability. Ability means meeting the quality and the schedule. Financial stability means the company is healthy with money and will not fail during the job. A credit check or a report from Dun and Bradstreet, a well-known provider of company financial information, can show this.

An RFQ focuses on price; the seller who meets quality and schedule at the lowest price usually wins. An RFP focuses more on meeting quality and schedule, though price still counts. Writing an RFP proposal is costly for sellers, so do not send it to companies that cannot win.
steps.example: For simple bolts that many shops sell, the team sends an RFQ and picks the lowest price. For a complex custom system, they send an RFP and study each seller's full plan.
keyPoints: An RFQ focuses on price; an RFP focuses on quality and schedule too
Bidders must be able to do the work and be financially stable
Qualify sellers before they bid, using lists or credit checks

steps.title: Quick check: RFQ vs RFP
steps.body: Let's make sure the two request types are clear.

quiz.question: What is the main focus of an RFQ (request for quote)?
options: The seller's full technical plan and approach
The price, for well-defined items from several sources
The long-term partnership relationship
The seller's marketing and brand
quiz.explanation: An RFQ focuses on price for well-defined goods or services, and the lowest qualified price usually wins.

steps.title: Evaluating bids and awarding the contract
steps.body:
Once bids arrive, you must evaluate them, which means judging which one is best. For RFQ bids on common items, price matters most. In most cases the lowest total price wins. Total price includes the goods, shipping costs, the value of any warranties, and any extra service that adds value.

For RFP bids the judging is harder. You look at price and also at the technical approach the seller chose. Your team must include people who understand the technical parts. On complex projects, one team scores the business part and another team scores the technical part. The two scores are combined to find the best proposal.

After picking the best value, a project representative checks all the terms of the bid and the contract with the seller. For simple awards, like printing, they just read and sign the contract so the seller understands the terms. For complex projects, they hold a detailed talk about goals, possible barriers, the schedule, key dates, and how to solve conflicts.

Awarding the contract is a careful final check. It makes sure both sides fully agree before the real work begins.
steps.example: Two teams score a complex bid: one checks the business terms, one checks the technical plan. Their scores are added, the best seller wins, and both sides meet to review goals.
keyPoints: RFQ bids are judged mostly on total price, including shipping and warranties
RFP bids are judged on price and technical approach together
Awarding means checking all terms so both sides fully agree before work starts

steps.title: Build it: manage the contract
steps.body: Let's put it together with a small task. Imagine you hired a seller to build a training course for your project. Write a short, simple plan (4 to 6 lines) for managing this contract. Try to include: (1) one metric you will track, such as on-time delivery or quality; (2) a progress-payment schedule tied to finished parts; (3) how you will handle a change, using a change order; and (4) one long-lead item you must order early. This mirrors real contract management, where you track performance, pay for finished work, manage changes, and watch items that take a long time to get. Managing the seller well is just as important to the project as the work your own team does.

title: Quality Planning
intro: Welcome to Quality Planning. In this module you will learn what "quality" really means on a project. It is not only about finishing on time and under budget. It is about building the right product, the way you promised, with few mistakes. You will learn how to plan quality before problems start, the difference between quality assurance and quality control, why quality costs money (and why it saves money too), and a few simple tools that help teams find and fix problems. The lessons use everyday examples, short quizzes, and small tasks so you can practice. Let's begin.

steps.title: What quality means on a project
steps.body:
Everybody thinks they know what "quality" means. But in project management, the word has a clear meaning. Quality means you build what you said you would build, and you build it well. You try not to make too many mistakes. You keep the team working toward the right goal.

Many people think a good project is only about time and money. That is not enough. You can finish early and spend little, but still make the wrong product. If the product does not fit your stakeholders' needs, the project is not a success.

Quality works like the triple constraint (scope, cost, and schedule). You manage it by setting goals and taking measurements. First you learn what quality level your stakeholders find acceptable. Then you make sure the project meets that level, just as it must meet the budget and the schedule.

So quality is not luck. It is a target you set and then check. When you plan quality, you decide how you will measure it and how you will stop mistakes before they grow.
steps.example: A team builds an app on time and under budget, but it crashes often and users hate it. It met the schedule, but it failed on quality because it did not do what the stakeholders needed.
keyPoints: Quality means building what you promised, and building it well.
On time and under budget is not enough by itself.
You manage quality by setting goals and taking measurements.
Learn the quality level your stakeholders accept, then meet it.

steps.title: Customer satisfaction and fitness to use
steps.body:
Two ideas help you understand project quality: customer satisfaction and fitness to use.

Customer satisfaction means the people who pay for the product are happy with what they get. When the team gathers requirements, they write down all the things the customer wants. This helps you know how to make the customer happy. Some requirements are not written down, but they are still real. These are "implied" needs. For example, a product that people hold should not be made from poison. Nobody writes that rule, but everyone expects it.

Fitness to use means the product has the best design to fit the customer's real needs. Ask yourself a question. Would you choose a beautiful product that does not do the job? Or an ugly product that does the job well? Most people choose the one that works. So a good product must do what it should do, and do it well.

Both ideas point to the same thing. The product must serve the real need, not just look nice.
steps.example: You can push a nail with a screwdriver, but a hammer fits the job better. The hammer has better "fitness to use" for that task.
keyPoints: Customer satisfaction: the people who pay are happy with the result.
Some needs are implied, not written, but still required (like safety).
Fitness to use: the design truly fits the customer's real need.
A working but plain product beats a pretty product that fails.

steps.title: Conformance to requirements
steps.body:
Conformance to requirements is the heart of quality. It means your product does what your requirements document says it should do. It is a measure of how well the product matches your plan.

Customer satisfaction and fitness to use both come together here. Your requirements should include what will make the customer happy and the best design for the job. So when you write requirements, think about both the stated needs and the implied needs. Stated needs are the ones the customer says out loud. Implied needs are the ones everyone expects, like safety or basic common sense.

In the end, the quality of your product is judged by one simple question. Did you build what you said you would build? If yes, you have good quality. If no, the product does not conform, even if it looks nice or was cheap to make.

This is why writing clear requirements matters so much. If the requirements are wrong or missing, the team may build the wrong thing perfectly. Good requirements give the team a clear target to hit.
steps.example: A requirements document says a bike light must shine for 10 hours. If the light lasts 10 hours, it conforms. If it lasts only 3 hours, it does not conform, even if it looks great.
keyPoints: Conformance means the product matches the requirements document.
Requirements should cover both stated and implied needs.
Quality is judged by: did you build what you said you would?
Clear requirements give the team a target to hit.

steps.title: Teach it back: fitness to use
steps.body: Now it is your turn to teach. Imagine a friend asks you: "What is the difference between customer satisfaction and fitness to use?" In your own words, explain both ideas. Use one simple example of your own, like a phone, a chair, or a tool. Try to show why a product that works but looks plain can still have high quality. Keep it short and clear, as if you were talking to someone new to project management.

steps.title: Quiz: what is quality?
steps.body: Let's check the main idea about quality on a project.

quiz.question: In project management, what is the best test of whether a product has good quality?
options: It was finished before the deadline
It cost less money than planned
It does what you said it would do, and does it well
It looks beautiful and modern
quiz.explanation: Quality means conformance to requirements: you built what you promised and built it well, not just fast, cheap, or pretty.

steps.title: Quality and grade are not the same
steps.body:
Quality and grade sound similar, but they mean different things. It helps to keep them apart.

The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) says quality is "the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfill requirements." In plain words, quality is how well something meets the requirements for its type. Grade is the category or level of a product. A grade tells you what to expect, and gives a basis to compare.

Here is the key point. A low-grade product can still have high quality. Even a cheap, low-grade product should work as expected, be safe, and last a reasonable time. So "low grade" does not mean "bad quality." It just means a lower level of features.

Quality is decided by how well the product meets the requirements of its own grade. A basic product that does its basic job well has high quality. A fancy product that fails to do its job has low quality, even at a high grade.
steps.example: Low-grade 87 octane gasoline that is clean and pure is high quality. High-grade 93 octane gasoline that is dirty with water is low quality. Grade is the level; quality is how well it meets its promise.
keyPoints: Grade is the category or level of a product.
Quality is how well it meets the requirements of that grade.
A low-grade product can still be high quality.
Low grade means fewer features, not bad quality.

steps.title: Teach it back: quality vs grade
steps.body: Explain the difference between quality and grade to someone who has never heard these words. Use a real product you know, like coffee, shoes, or a laptop. Show one example of a low-grade item that is still high quality, and one high-grade item that is low quality. This helps you remember that grade is the level, and quality is how well the item keeps its promise.

steps.title: What quality planning is
steps.body:
Quality planning happens early, at the start of the project. It means taking all the information you have and deciding how you will measure quality and how you will prevent defects. A defect is a mistake or fault in the product.

Most companies have a quality policy. This is a written rule that says how the company measures quality across all its work. Your project should follow this company policy. You should also follow any government rules or laws that apply to your project.

Good quality planning has a few clear steps. First, decide which activities you will use to measure quality. Second, think about the cost of all these quality activities. Third, set the guidelines, or standards, that you will measure against. Fourth, design the tests you will run when the product is ready.

The big idea is simple. You get high quality by planning for it, not by reacting to problems after they appear. When you choose standards early and put good processes in place, you stop many defects before they happen.
steps.example: Before building an online course, the team writes standards for each screen, plans a review after every module, and designs a final test. They plan quality before writing a single lesson.
keyPoints: Quality planning happens early in the project.
Follow the company quality policy and any government rules.
Steps: choose activities, count their cost, set standards, design tests.
Plan for quality; do not just react to problems later.

steps.title: Measuring quality: control limits and tolerance
steps.body:
When you make the same product many times, you measure samples to check quality. A sample is a small part taken from the whole group. Measuring every single item would be too slow, so you test samples instead.

Every process has small changes you cannot fully control. So measurements will not be exactly the same each time. To handle this, you set control limits. Control limits are the highest and lowest values you allow. If a sample falls between them, it passes.

The space between the upper and lower limit is called the tolerance. People often write tolerance as the target value, plus or minus an amount, using the sign ±. For example, if the target is 87 and the limits are 86 and 88, the tolerance is 87 ± 1.

Small tolerance means you allow very little change. That gives tighter control, but it usually costs more to produce and to measure. So you must balance how tight you need to be against the cost. Sometimes a wider tolerance is fine and much cheaper.
steps.example: A refinery decides its 87 octane fuel must test between 86 and 88. Any sample inside that range passes. The tolerance is 87 ± 1.
keyPoints: A sample is a small part taken to represent the whole.
Control limits are the highest and lowest allowed values.
Tolerance is the range between the limits, shown as value ± amount.
Tighter tolerance gives more control but usually costs more.

steps.title: Quiz: tolerance
steps.body: You learned that tolerance is the range between the control limits. Let's check it.

quiz.question: A part must measure between 48 mm and 52 mm. How would you write this tolerance?
quiz.explanation: The target (middle) is 50 mm and each limit is 2 mm away, so the tolerance is 50 ± 2.

steps.title: The cost of quality
steps.body:
Quality is not free. The cost of quality (COQ) is the total cost of everything you do to make sure the product is good. This is more than just testing.

Cost of quality includes many activities. It includes writing standards, reviewing documents, and holding meetings to find the root cause of a defect. It includes reworking to fix defects after the team finds them. In short, it is everything you do to ensure quality on the project. When you add all of this up, you get the cost of quality.

Why track this number? Because it tells you if your project is doing well or having trouble. If your company records the cost of quality on every project, you can compare. You can see if you are spending more or less than usual to reach quality standards.

Remember that tolerance affects cost too. Very small tolerances cost more, and the extra cost is not always worth it. If checking every step costs more than fixing problems later, the cost of quality may be too high. Then you may accept a few more defects.
steps.example: A team's cost of quality includes writing standards, reviewing screens, root-cause meetings, and rework. If checking each screen costs more than fixing issues after launch, they allow more small defects to keep COQ reasonable.
keyPoints: Cost of quality (COQ) is everything you do to ensure quality.
It includes standards, reviews, meetings, testing, and rework.
Tracking COQ helps you compare projects and spot trouble.
Very tight tolerances raise COQ and may not be worth it.

steps.title: Teach it back: cost of quality
steps.body: A teammate says: "Quality only costs us money, so let's skip it." In your own words, explain what the cost of quality really includes, and why spending on quality can actually save money. Mention rework and finding defects early. Use a simple example from a project you can imagine. Keep your explanation short, friendly, and clear.

steps.title: Quality assurance vs quality control
steps.body:
Two ideas often get mixed up: quality control and quality assurance. They work together but are not the same.

Quality control happens during the work. The team samples and measures the actual products and services to see if they are inside the control limits. This work is often done by a separate quality control group. They check the real output and look for the causes of any variation. So quality control looks at the product itself.

Quality assurance is different. Its purpose is to build confidence that the quality plan and controls are working properly. The team sets aside time to review the original quality plan. Then they compare that plan to how quality is actually being handled during the project. So quality assurance looks at the process and the plan, not just the product.

Quality assurance can be done inside the company as an internal review. It can also be done by an outside group in an audit. An audit is a careful, independent check. Both build trust that standards and steps are truly being followed.
steps.example: Quality control: a team measures samples of fuel to see if each is between 86 and 88 octane. Quality assurance: managers review whether the quality plan itself is being followed correctly, sometimes through an outside audit.
keyPoints: Quality control checks the actual product against the limits.
Quality assurance checks that the plan and controls are working.
QC looks at the product; QA looks at the process and plan.
QA can be an internal review or an external audit.

steps.title: Quiz: QA vs QC
steps.body: Let's make sure you can tell quality assurance and quality control apart.

quiz.question: A group measures product samples to check they are within the control limits. Which activity is this?
options: Quality assurance
Quality control
Quality planning
Benchmarking
quiz.explanation: Measuring the actual product samples against the limits is quality control; quality assurance instead reviews whether the plan and controls are working.

steps.title: Quality planning tools: cost-benefit, benchmarking, and tests
steps.body:
Project managers have several tools to plan and control quality. You do not use every tool on every project. You choose based on how complex the project is and what the client's quality program needs. Here are three common tools.

Cost-benefit analysis compares how much your quality activities will cost against how much you will gain. Costs are easy to measure, like the time and resources you spend. Benefits are harder to see, because quality work does not make a product by itself. But the benefits are real: less rework, higher productivity, and happier team members and customers.

Benchmarking means using results from other projects to set goals for yours. Maybe the last project had 20% fewer defects than the one before. You should learn from that project and copy the ideas that worked. Benchmarks give you reference points before you even start.

Design of experiments is the list of all the tests you plan to run on your product. It lists the test procedures and approaches, and even the tests themselves. In software, this is often called test planning.
steps.example: Before a project starts, a manager checks that last year's team cut defects by 20% (benchmarking), lists every test she will run (design of experiments), and weighs the cost of reviews against the value they bring (cost-benefit analysis).
keyPoints: Cost-benefit analysis weighs quality costs against the gains.
Benefits of quality: less rework, more productivity, happier people.
Benchmarking copies good results from other projects.
Design of experiments is your full plan of tests to run.

steps.title: Simple tools to find and rank problems
steps.body:
Some tools help you find the cause of a problem and decide which problem to fix first. Here are four useful ones.

A cause-and-effect diagram, also called a fishbone diagram, helps a team discover why a defect happens. The team draws a shape like a fish skeleton. Each bone is a possible cause, such as people, machines, or power. The team talks and adds ideas to each branch. This makes hidden causes easier to see.

A check sheet is a simple form. Each time a problem happens, the user makes a check mark in the right box. This is an easy way to collect data about how often each problem occurs.

A histogram turns that data into a column chart. Each column is a category, and the height shows how many times that problem happened. It helps you see the pattern at a glance.

A Pareto chart is a special histogram. The columns are placed in order, from most common on the left to least common on the right. A line shows the running total. It helps you see which few problems cause most of the trouble, so you fix those first.
steps.example: A factory team builds a fishbone diagram and finds six possible causes of a defect. They use a check sheet to count each problem, then a Pareto chart to see that two problems cause most of the defects, so they start there.
keyPoints: Fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram: find possible causes of a defect.
Check sheet: a simple form to count how often problems happen.
Histogram: a column chart showing how often each problem occurs.
Pareto chart: columns in order, so you fix the biggest problems first.

steps.title: Build a fishbone diagram
steps.body: Pick a real problem you know, for example "my morning is always late" or "our team meetings run too long." Draw a simple fishbone diagram. Write the problem on the right as the fish head. Then draw four to six bones. Label each bone with a category of possible cause, such as People, Tools, Time, or Environment. Under each bone, list one or two specific causes. Keep it simple. The goal is to practice finding many possible causes instead of guessing at just one.

steps.title: Rank problems with a Pareto idea
steps.body: Imagine you tracked defects for one week using a check sheet and got these counts: Late delivery 12, Wrong color 8, Missing part 25, Scratched surface 3, Wrong size 2. Put these in Pareto order, from the most common problem to the least common. Then decide which one or two problems you would fix first, and write one sentence explaining why. This shows how a Pareto view helps you spend effort where it matters most.

steps.title: Meeting client expectations
steps.body:
Clients give you specifications, which are written requirements the project must meet. Meeting these specifications is one clear sign of project success. But clients also have expectations that are hard to write down. If you miss these hidden expectations, the client may feel unhappy even when you met the written specs.

For example, one client wants to be invited to every meeting, then picks the ones that matter to them. Another client only wants invites to meetings that truly need their input. If you invite this second client to everything, you will annoy them. So you must listen and learn what each client really expects.

Project surveys are a helpful tool here. A survey asks the client how they feel the project is going. The answers give the team useful data. If a survey shows the client is unhappy about something, the team can talk about it early and make a recovery plan. A recovery plan is a set of actions to fix the problem. Surveys also show what is going well and what needs improvement.

Listening early keeps small worries from becoming big problems.
steps.example: A client seems quiet and unhappy. A short project survey shows they feel left out of decisions. The team adds a weekly update and invites them to key meetings, fixing the issue before it grows.
keyPoints: Specifications are the written requirements you must meet.
Clients also have expectations that are hard to write down.
Listen to each client to learn their real, unstated needs.
Surveys reveal problems early so you can make a recovery plan.

steps.title: Quiz: quality tools
steps.body: You have met several quality tools. Let's check that you can match a tool to its job.

quiz.question: You want to see which few problems happen most often so you can fix them first. Which tool is best?
options: A fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram
A Pareto chart
A quality policy
A recovery plan
quiz.explanation: A Pareto chart orders problems from most to least common with a running total, so you can quickly see and fix the biggest ones first.

title: Communication Planning
intro: Welcome! In this module you will learn about communication planning — one of the biggest parts of a project manager's job. In fact, about 90% of a PM's time is spent on communication. You will learn how to make a communication plan, how to choose the right channels and methods, how to write status reports, and how to manage the flow of information so the right people get the right message at the right time. We keep the language simple and use real examples. Let's begin!

steps.title: Communication is the heart of the PM job
steps.body:
Let's start with a surprising fact. About 90% of a project manager's time is spent on communication. That is almost the whole job! Communication management means keeping everybody "in the loop." This means making sure people know what is happening on the project.

Good communication is about giving the right message to the right person at the right time. If people do not get the information they need, they cannot make good decisions. Small mistakes in communication can grow into big problems.

When we plan communication, we answer four simple questions:

- What types of information will we share?
- Who will receive this information?
- What format (form) will we use, like email or a report?
- When will we send it?

Think of yourself as the center of the project. Information comes to you from many people. Then you must pass the right pieces to the right people. This is a big and important part of your work.
keyPoints: About 90% of a PM's time goes to communication.
The goal: right message, right person, right time.
Plan the what, who, format, and when of every message.
steps.example: A PM learns that a supplier will be two days late. She quickly tells the team leader and the client, but she does not need to tell every single worker. She shares the news with the people who must act on it.

steps.title: Communications requirements analysis
steps.body:
Before you send anything, you must find out what your stakeholders really need. Stakeholders are the people who care about the project or are affected by it, like the client, the boss, and the team.

This first step is called communications requirements analysis. It just means figuring out what kind of information each stakeholder needs so they can make good decisions.

Here is the tricky part. Your project will produce a huge amount of information. You should not send all of it to everyone. If you do, you will "bury" your stakeholders in too much information, and they will miss the important parts. But you also must give them enough, so they stay informed.

Also remember: valuable information is not always good news. Sometimes you must share bad news too, like a delay or a risk. You do not always paint a "rosy" (too positive) picture. Honesty helps people trust you and make smart choices.

So your job is to find the balance. Not too much, not too little. Just the information each person truly values.
keyPoints: Requirements analysis = finding out what info each stakeholder needs.
Do not overload people; do not starve them either.
Share bad news too, not only good news.
steps.example: The company director only wants a short monthly summary of cost and schedule. The team leader, however, needs daily updates on tasks. Same project, but different information needs.

steps.title: Quick check: how much communication?
steps.body: Let's check one key idea before we move on.

quiz.question: Roughly how much of a project manager's time is spent on communication?
options: About 10%
About 50%
About 90%
Almost none
quiz.explanation: The chapter says about 90% of a PM's job is communication, which is why planning it well matters so much.

steps.title: Quick check: too much information
steps.body: Think about the balance we just discussed.

quiz.question: What is the risk of sending stakeholders every piece of project information?
options: They will trust you more
They may be overwhelmed and miss what is important
The project will finish faster
They will never need meetings
quiz.explanation: Too much information buries stakeholders, so they can miss the parts that matter for good decisions.

steps.title: Choosing your communication technology
steps.body:
There are many ways to share information: written reports, phone calls, email, formal status reports, meetings, online databases, shared schedules, and project websites. Which ones should you pick?

The chapter gives you several factors to think about before you choose:

- Timing: How often do people need updates? Some news must travel fast.
- Systems: Do you already have tools that work, or must you buy (procure) new technology?
- Staff experience: Do your team and stakeholders already know how to use this technology, or must you train them first?
- Project duration and environment: Will the tool still work for the whole life of the project? And where is your team? Are they in one place, or spread across many locations?

The last point is important today. Many teams work far apart. Their tools must connect people who are not in the same room.

Whatever you decide, you write your answers to these questions inside the communication plan. That way, everyone knows how information will move.
keyPoints: Methods include email, reports, meetings, websites, and calls.
Consider timing, existing systems, staff skill, and project length.
Location of the team shapes which tools work best.
steps.example: A team spread across three cities chooses a shared online project website plus weekly video calls, instead of paper reports that would arrive too slowly.

steps.title: Your turn: explain the factors
steps.body: Teach it back in your own words. Imagine a friend is starting their first project. Explain the four things they should think about before choosing a communication method (timing, existing systems, staff experience, and project duration/location). Use simple words and one small example of your own.
keyPoints: Name each factor in plain language.
Say why each one matters.
Add a short example to make it real.
steps.example: You might say: 'First, ask how fast people need news. Second, check what tools you already have. Third, ask if the team can use the tool or needs training. Fourth, think about how long the project runs and where people sit.'

steps.title: What the communication plan documents
steps.body:
Every project needs a solid communication plan. But not every project uses the same types of communication or the same methods. So each plan is a little different.

A communication plan writes down three main things:

- The types of information the stakeholders need.
- When the information should be sent.
- How the information will be delivered.

That's the core: what, when, and how.

The types of information you usually share include: project status, the scope statement and its updates, baseline information (the original plan you measure against), risks, action items, performance measures, and project acceptance.

Here is a smart tip from the chapter. Decide the information needs as early as possible in the planning phase. Why? Because when you create a document, you will already know who should get a copy and how to send it. This saves time and avoids confusion later.
keyPoints: The plan records what info, when, and how it is delivered.
Common contents: status, scope, risks, action items, and more.
Decide information needs early in planning.
steps.example: During week one of planning, the PM lists that the client wants a status report every Friday by email, and that risks go to the sponsor as soon as they appear. Both facts go into the communication plan.

steps.title: Two big families of communication
steps.body:
To complete a project well, team members must communicate clearly. If people work in the same building, this is easy. They can hold regular meetings, stop by each other's desk for a quick answer, or chat during a coffee break.

But many teams do not share a building. They work mostly through electronic tools like email and video. We call these virtual teams. "Virtual" here means they meet through technology, not face to face.

Virtual teams have a special risk. Without normal daily contact, small misunderstandings can grow and harm trust. People may also feel left out of the team culture. So a virtual team needs a clear plan to communicate reliably and on time.

This planning starts by understanding two major types of communication. In the next steps we will meet both:

- Synchronous communication (people talk at the same time).
- Asynchronous communication (people do not need to be present at the same time).

Understanding these two families helps you pick the right tool for each situation.
keyPoints: Same-building teams talk easily and informally.
Virtual teams meet through technology and need a clear plan.
Two families: synchronous and asynchronous.
steps.example: A design team with members in India and Canada is a virtual team. They never meet in person, so they agree on clear rules for updates to keep trust strong.

steps.title: Synchronous communication
steps.body:
Communication is synchronous when all the people take part at the same time. "Synchronous" means happening together, at the same moment. A phone call is a simple example: both people are on the line right now.

The chapter lists several types of synchronous communication:

- Live meeting: team members gather in the same place.
- Conference call: a phone call with several people.
- Audio conference: like a conference call, but online with software.
- Computer-assisted conference: an audio conference plus a shared document or spreadsheet both sides can edit.
- Video conference: like an audio conference but with live video of the people.
- Instant messaging (IM): quick text or voice messages in pop-up windows.
- Texting: short text messages between phones.

Modern tools let you build a team from anywhere in the world. That is powerful. But there is one catch: time zones. Most people work during the day. If team members are in very different time zones, finding a shared time can be hard.

Sometimes this is even useful. We will see how in a moment.
keyPoints: Synchronous = everyone takes part at the same time.
Examples: live meetings, calls, video calls, IM, texting.
Time zones can make live meetings hard to schedule.
steps.example: A Monday video conference where the whole team joins at 3 p.m. and talks live is synchronous communication.

steps.title: Asynchronous communication
steps.body:
Getting everyone together at the same moment can be hard, especially across time zones. Luckily, many messages do not need people to be present at the same time. This is called asynchronous communication. "Asynchronous" means not at the same time.

You send a message now, and the other person reads and answers later, when it suits them. The chapter describes several choices:

- Mail and package delivery: useful when you need original signed contracts. Overnight delivery can speed this up.
- Fax: old, but still trusted. In many countries a faxed signed contract is legal, while a scanned image is not.
- Email: very common. You can send to a whole list, save messages as a record, and attach files.
